text: This video has not aged well, if you search YouTube for Canadian news clips, Canada is in crisis mode, still years after Covid, for wait times, accessibility and availability. All of this comes from the government budgeting total health care costs. Yes, hospitals are given an annual budget and that causes quotas and rationing, but that is just the start. Quotas also are set on how many family doctors and specialists will be trained in Canadian med schools. Even doctor residency programs have quotas. So even in the private provider sector, costs are controlled by artificially planned scarcity. Canadian specialists are not allowed to perform common in-office surgeries like cataract surgery or colonoscopies, they have to make use of the surgery rooms in overcrowded hospitals and that means both the specialist and patient must wait many months to get scheduled in. It is start-of-finish centrally planned at the provincial level and all ten provinces act alone in their endeavors leading to ten microcosm "command economy" health care systems all performing on their own and doing terrible jobs. Wait times are in fact life threatening to patients, Canada isn't spending "little" on health care, they actually are the top spenders of the universal health care nations, also change is very difficult and slow in Canada because of the very nature of the system.

text: In BC and probably the others too, there are two levels of healthcare.  If you have private insurance with an employer, union. or the like, you get better care than those who have no coverage beyond what the government provides. For example, a senior with a broken bone will go to the hospital and it will be set in a cast, if appropriate, a few x-rays taken, and all the other first responder services. The hospital will then take a few more x-rays and remove the cast. If further help is needed, and it usually is, the patient will pay for his own physiotherapist, or other specialist help. If a patient has no private insurance and ends up in palliative care, there is a dorm "down there" for them and better accommodation and care for those who have insurance.  The list could probably go on and talk about other issues, but these examples are those of which I have FIRST HAND KNOWLEDGE. My friend died in palliative care and it was the most depressing place imaginable, but my when my neighbor, who had insurance, died in the same place, his family sang the praises of the place and the nurses and doctors. I know a senior with insurance who was cared for at the hospital by a physiotherapist and another who had no insurance who was referred to a physiotherapist at his own expense. If a patient has insurance, the hospitals can milk it. If the patient has no insurance, they can only milk the patient's savings, which may be non-existent. That's BC and Canada today.

text:
Canada should not be any country's beacon for a health care system. Most Canadians themselves don't realize that their country is now the only country on earth that makes it illegal for its citizens to purchase private health insurance for ALL services covered by their Provinces Medicare. The healthcare system is now being taken to the Supreme Court of Canada as it breaks the Charter of Rights and Freedoms for life, liberty... It happened already in 2005 in Quebec vs Chaoulli and now it will soon happen with BC's Cambie Surgeries. The system is overwhelmed, broken and inefficient with Gov't rationing of everything.  There are historic levels of people dying on waitlists or becoming addicted to drugs while they wait for their surgeries. 15% of Canadians cannot find a family GP.

Dual citizen here: born and raised in Canada and I used to be so proud of our UHC system but have since lived in Taiwan, S Korea, Ireland and now the States.  Taiwan and S Korea are always at the top of the charts for the worlds best health care system. However, they (and every other nation) allow a private sector to FULLY function alongside the public. It helps to prevent overwhelming the system. 

I have since come to my senses and have learned first hand how much of a crisis it currently is in Canada. I'm genuinely happy when I hear of other peoples successful cancer stories, but my mother's isn't one of them. She passed away in Oct 2021 after being diagnosed with cancer in the summer. She died without seeing an Oncologist ONCE and without even a prognosis of how much time she had left, so she and all of us could get our heads around it. The diagnostic imaging, endoscopies, and tests were ridiculously far apart due to waitlists. At one point, when trying to get an earlier CT scan, the receptionist told me there were over 4000 people on the waistlist. So crazy as she was an RN her entire adult life in BC; paid exorbitant taxes for 5 DECADES faithfully -  into an inefficient and broken system that wasn't there for her when she needed it most. It's not just her. Since sharing her story, I'm now connecting with so many others with similar horrendous experiences. 

It seems to be a knee-jerk reaction when Canadians hear the two words: healthcare and privatization-  they immediately think of the States. Rest assured, Canada will never get to the level of privatization of the States. It will more likely follow the a Sweden, Taiwan, or UK model. All countries with hybrid systems.  Assuming Cambie surgeries wins, I hope Canadians will be allowed to purchase private health insurance for ALL services provided by their provinces medicare. You earn your hard earned dollars from your job and it should be YOUR choice how to spend it. If you choose to spend it on a health insurance company or to get a procedure done because the gov't is too slow - that should be your choice.

text:
Not sure if it's just Nova Scotia, but family doctors don't have fees for most services.  Sadly here the funding seems low, they got rid of a lot of our mental health services and I know many people who severly need the support.  Our town protested but it happened anyway. A young man in my town killed himself because the hospital simply sent him away.  My brothers gf is now going through the same thing, a suicide attempt and the hospital let her go the next day even though she said she'd try again.
Also, our doctors/nurses are overworked and overlook or simply don't have enough time to find out peoples issues!  My friends mom was sent home and told she was just constipated but she really had flesh eating disease and died only a few days later.  At least making more money motivates doctors to really find the issue.  Similar issue happened to my aunt, who later found out she had bowel cancer and she also passed away.  Both were literally sent away and told to use metamucil.
I think this is just a Nova Scotia issue, since we're one of the smaller provinces and not enough resources are allocated our way.  Most family doctors are retiring and no one to replace them (seems like any everywhere problem).

Sorry my comment is gloomy, and I've had lots of positive experiences with our health care, but I watch tragic things happen to people and it seems as if there's not much for us to do about it.

text:
Ok this is driving me nuts seeing the love for Canada’s healthcare system. 
Canadian here from ont. 
each province is different . I need health insurance to go to Quebec for treatment . The west is becoming very privatized . My sister lives in bc and she needs blue cross coverage to go to the doctor
In Ontario much of what was covered when I was a kid like Physiotherapy and other services like eye exam are no longer covered. Canada is slowly but surely going private . 
Ontario will pay for an abortion but not cover a severe diabetic! 
I cannot go to any public hospital in Ontario and try to purchase healthcare. Not legal I have to use Ohip system. But anyone outside of Ontario can come in and pay cash. Since our hospitals  work on for profit ! Called tourist medicine . I experienced this first. It was thought I had a brain bleed and was in need of a ct scan and put in triage. but was jumped in the queue by a European couple paying cash that scan for non life threatening issue. 
This happens in many other places.
Now for my gp I belong to a private healthcare group that bills the govt or me. Much much better care and treatment. But hospitals nope fully socialized. The wait times are usually due to our doctor shortage . Thousands have no family doctor in Canada ! Huge problem! So people got to the hospital to get refills . A cough a cold so on. Hence why wait times are usually 4 hours min. Plus you need an advocate when in hospital to fight for you as the patient . Doctors generally don’t listen to patients that often in hospitals . So you have someone there to ask and speak on your behave. This is advice from gp and others in medical field.
Most Canadians will praise our system . They will say yah I was seen and got this done . But if it’s small yes it will get done. May take a while, but if you have a catastrophic illness LIKE I DID YOU BETTER HAVE INSURANCE! 
Now mind you we have great doctors and some great medicine .  But our system is VERY POLITICIZED and broken in many ways .
The video didn’t lie but it’s not telling the truth either .

text:
I'm a 28 year old Canadian with Fibromyalgia, who has been in 3 car accidents and broke a lot of bones as a kid. I've had to undergo more tests than I can remember, had a fair amount of ER visits, and a LOT of doctor's office visits. I know Americans like to demonize our healthcare system, but I have never had to pay out of pocket or been denied anything, and I haven't been forced to wait for medical tests. I think it's hard for Americans to understand, because they think that a single-payer system would somehow cost them more money. Ours is paid for by our taxes (which I don't think are unfairly high), and I didn't know what a co-pay was until I started learning about the US system on Youtube. What I would improve is to add (at least) prescription cost coverage for life-threatening conditions, like cancers. I know it's been talked about (for children), at least, and that is one area we should improve on.

I don't know anyone who is truly unhappy with our healthcare system, and I don't know of a single person who has gone to the US for medical care that was non-elective. In fact, the people I know who have been forced to seek emergency care while on vacation to the US will usually move heaven and earth to somehow get their medical attention in Canada, as even with travel insurance the cost could bankrupt them. I've seen more GoFundMe pages about this than I would like. I do know quite a few Americans who came to Canada for medical treatment and medications. No system is perfect, and we know ours isn't, but there's no need to attack it, especially when so many Americans are aware that their system isn't working for them.
video_title: Canada's Healthcare System Explained!

text: so I both like and dislike the assessment of the Canadian health system presented here, specifically in regards to wait times. Canada spends a similar amount of our GDP on healthcare as most European countries but in surveys of European single payer systems and in surveys of commonwealth single payer systems Canada really does rank near the bottom for wait times. It isn't a myth. Our wait times are probably quite similar to US wait times but that doesn't mean they are good. The world is bigger than our two countries. Now, there are lots of reasons for wait time issues like it being an average of all of Canada where a significant portion of the country is very spread out with a low population and thus, distances has a significant impact. I mean there are places in Canada where the closest hospital is hours away while that just isn't a problem in the UK because the country is physically smaller and thus faster to travel to and that counts in the average. Distances and low population affects resources. Less bang for your buck, etc. BUT that doesn't account for the wait times in some of the most densely population places in the country like Toronto, a city of 3 million people with a catchment area (aka commuter zone) that covers over 5 million people. Go to the hospital on a Saturday and unless you are imminently dying expect to wait 6 or 8 hours to see a doctor. That is just unacceptable. The NHS in the UK would have a wait time of less than half that. And distance and population density does not account for that. We really do have longer than average wait times for single payer systems and that can cause problems. Canada also has a shortage of general practitioners and in some places it can take months if not years to get taken on as a patient and even when you are a patient the doctor may be too booked to see you when you need an appointment so you may need to go to a walk in clinic anyway when you are sick. (the doctors make a monthly fee for each of their patients and then also make a fee for services provided for every appointment they have. If you go to a walk in because your doctor is too busy to see you they get a fine taken off their monthly fee for you. This is to encourage doctors to actually be able to see the patients they are getting paid to see). TL;DR Canada may be similar to the US but our wait times are actually longer than average for common wealth and European single payer systems even after you have account for issues like physical distance and low population density.
video_title: Canada's Healthcare System Explained!

Although the wait times for some procedures in Canada may be much longer due to the amount of people needing them and the amount of providers available to give them, I still can’t see people travelling to the US just to pay 100% more for something they could have for free or near free by either waiting for it in their home town or having to pay a much smaller fee for it in one of the private providers there who can get people in and out with a shorter wait time than the public care. If they were going to travel to get better care i could understand the travel to get to a specialist with an opening, but just leaving to get quicker care I can only see people travelling to a private practice with specialized pricing in their own country before travelling here to pay the full price of the procedure regardless of what the wait time would be. I like that they have the option for private providers also. The fact that they give options to people to go through a different practice if they want shows that they have freedom. Even though some people feel like it’s unfair because not everyone has the financial means to get private care but I feel like it’s the same as anything in life, everyone can’t afford everything and there’s nothing unfair about the fact that someone else has more money than other, especially when there is still the same quality of care waiting there for the ones that don’t have the money.

text: I've never needed emergency care growing up (god bless) and don't get sick very often, but from the times I do need to see a doctor, I just pop by with my health care card (sometimes I don't even need my card if I'm already in their system) and wait a bit. Wait times can be long, upwards of 30-45 minutes for a 15-25 minute check up. I don't mind though since I just drop in quite unannounced. I've never paid for over-the-counter drugs when I was a student and luckily, the medication I do need sometimes (tylenol, cough syrups, nasal congestion) are rather cheap in my opinion- maybe as high as 15 dollars and as low as 4 dollars (CAD). I do want to point out that some of the services mentioned in the video like optometry and dental do not entirely come out of pocket or supplementary insurance (like work or school). Children (up to ages 18) are fully covered for eye examinations once a year. Seniors, diabetics, those with really high prescriptions, low income, Native status and people with very specific health complications often have their eye examinations subsidized or covered completely (depends on the clinic and their practice). Unfortunately, there are not nearly as much coverage for dental services, though I don't know the numbers. Our system was about 70/30 public-private when I was in school, but public spending was going down from 70. Ambulances in some places will charge you now, which use to be entirely free. We have a reasonable number of physicians in proportion to our population, but many are general practitioners (not enough specialists) and a big majority have a big preference to work in cities (rural areas are less serviced, accessibility suffers). There's always that debate in regards to fee-for-service and rostering (as well as other methods), though a fee-for-service does make a little more sense for our system (I believe it was about 40-60 dollars for each 15 minute check-ups for a general practitioner, charged to medicare). Quite a number of kinks in our system still and not the best, but I quite like it so far.

I'd like to chime in on the wait time thing. Finding a specialist for something that is not immediately life threatening is what takes a bit of time. I have Crohn's, looking for the gastro took a few weeks to book a time to see him, then another few to see the surgeon. This was for diagnosis and again, not immediately life threatening. A while later a fistula formed and basically every appointment was a week or two for scopes and seeing specialist. The initial scheduled operation was 2 months out, but was on a priority list if cancellation happened. Effective wait time from the decision to be operated on to wheeling into the operating room was 2 weeks due to a cancellation.

Later on a second fistula formed in a rather secluded/complicated area and was going through the usual week or two waits to see specialist when the pain spiked hard one night. Got myself to the hospital/ER that night at around 10pm and was assessed and on morphine within 20 minutes. Ultrasound and MRIs were done within 2 hours, operation was planned the next morning, and I was in the operating room in the afternoon. 

Overall a pretty good experience in terms of getting care. All they needed was my health card. No worrying about affiliated hospitals, insurance restrictions, or insane fees and crippling debt.

text:
"Optional MRI?" Try living under the Canadian healthcare system.  My husband has been living in chronic pain for 10+ years.  It took 5 or 6 years to get surgery.  He's waiting for an MRI right now.  It's in April 2016 AND that's provided he doesn't get bumped for an emergency.  He received the date in August 2015.  

To get the MRI appointment, he had to actually jump through hoops.  Even though he's had a previous MRI and surgery to show what the problem is, he's had to spend out of pocket money on physio AND prescription pain killers over a period of 2 years before the MRI appointment would be accepted.  Now, if he doesn't jump through more hoops to prove he's actually suffering, he won't receive an appointment with a specialist.  More hoops = more out of pocket spending for us.  The MRI is to see how much worse the condition has gotten.

Did I mention that our previous doctor didn't tell us that the surgery received 6 years ago did not fix 100% of his problem?  We didn't know they only hit one part of a multi-part issue.  He also did not receive post-surgery follow-ups or instructions.  Our current doctor is furious but there isn't much he can do short of prescribing pain killers and hoping he can get my husband in ASAP.

Ya... great system, if you're healthy.

text:
Yes, sometimes you need to get treatment elsewhere, BUT if for any reason you need to see a particular specialist who is out of province or out of the country, your provincial government will pay for it all. This happens all the time when residents in smaller provinces or territories need to see a specialist in a larger city centre. People in the Maritimes and people up north in the territories have to fly down (all paid by the government) usually to Ontario, Alberta, or BC. They will even pay the plane ticket for someone to accompany you to get medical treatment elsewhere. That's just a very practical solution as you are not going to have every expensive equipment or specialist if your population is only 30,000 (Nunavut), or 150,000 (PEI). 

My boyfriend had to do this in Ontario too though. There was a particular American colleague his Ontario/Canadian specialist wanted him to see in San Diego, California and the Ontario government paid everything.

text: [Big fan here, by the way! Keep up the good work!] I scrolled down to about comment 400 but didn't see if anyone clarified a few minor facts. (1) This one is more of a nitpick since commenters seem to be confused--we Canadians actually do spend quite a bit on health care, though it's funded in part by taxes so it isn't a purely government investment. (2) Fertility treatments are not covered in any province but Quebec (and only as of 2010), with 1 or 2 other provinces allowing tax deductions for fertility treatment expenses (I think Manitoba and Saskatchewan). (3) While federal negotiations for drugs and devices do help keep the prices down, it is up to the provinces to decide which drugs and devices they will cover. (4) Regarding "medical tourism," thank you so much for touching on that, since it's become a popular topic in recent years. Despite media reports touting huge numbers of patients asking health care providers for advice or recommendations for such services, the recorded numbers who do seek treatment in specific areas have been quite low [see PMID:20149916 for example]. However, it's nearly impossible to determine actual numbers because information is dependent on patients volunteering information that is usually not requested of them (similar to issues in tracking naturopathic treatment use). (5) And finally, regarding wait times: there will always be wait times of some sort for surgery (even non-elective), modern diagnostics, family physicians, organ donation, etc. However, take into account that some reports of long wait times are due to Canada's geography; we have only 35 million people who live in a gigantic country. Our biggest cities are separated by hundreds of kilometers, and that's assuming you even live within spitting distance of a major city. We've got some top-notch medical and research hospitals, though! [As a final aside, we hear all your media reports of anti-Obamacare and whatnot, but in the 50 or so years we've had our "socialized medicine," how often have you heard Canadians ask to get rid of it? I'd venture a guess of never.]

Wait times may be a by-product of financial means, but what most people in Canada (from what I've seen as an EMR) don't understand is how the triage system works. This is where the charge nurse labels you based on your issue's severity and then continues admitting people with more serious injuries. If you come in with a collapsed lung, you're not going to be waiting. If you're left to wait, it's actually a good thing.

The wait times that we're considering to be a real problem are elective and non-life threatening. Not emergency. If you have a deviated septum that means you can't fully breathe out of your nose, you're going to wait a month or two. It isn't going to harm you or cause any life threatening issues.

text:
The fact that Medicare here only pays for the health care of seniors is actually one of the main reasons we spend so much more. In Canada, if you're 65+, your health care is rationed, partly to keep suppliers available for younger people who can benefit more from preventative care. In the US, if you're 65+, you're first in line to get health care. Our spending here on people age 18-65 is about the same as Canada and most European countries. Our spending on people age 65-75 is twice as high, and our spending on people age 75+ is three times as high.

text:
Thank you for making this video. As a future physician, I aspire to be like you and promote science and healthcare education. 

Our system in Canada is not perfect but I do think it is pretty great. Based on my experience, one of the bigger issues with Canadian healthcare (especially in Quebec, where I'm from) is that there are quite a few people who don't have family physicians (Quebec has the highest percentage of people without a family physician: 25%, Ontario has the lowest, ~8%). Because of this, people often go to emergency rooms for primary care treatments. Not only does this contribute to increasing wait times but it actually costs the government 3 times more than it would if they were seeing a family physician instead.

Another thing about wait times in Canada is that they can often be avoided if you have the money and/or insurance to go to the private sector for the service. For instance, my mother has chronic back pain needed an MRI and waited ~9 months to get the MRI (public sector). This was completely covered by Medicare but if she wanted she could have gone to a private clinic and had it done much earlier. In a private clinic however, it wouldn't be covered by medicare or our health insurance so she would have to pay $1000 for the MRI. It's unfortunate that having more money could mean easier access to health care. Still, I'm proud of the fact that in Canada, no one is in debt because of medical care and everyone has access to treatment.

Thank you HT!!  As a Canadian I am proud to see our system explained by Americans in a positive light.  It is such a relief to be a Canadian and not have to worry about health costs unless expensive drug plans are necessary.  I have had many family members and friends go through the system for medical procedures such as lower back surgery, hip replacements, knee replacements and other things, none have complained about our system and all felt they were treated well.  Particularly in cases where surgery was necessary due to severe pain and medical emergencies, these people were pushed through the system remarkably fast in order to get them better and allow for them to get back to work.  

I am glad to live in a country where I feel secure that if I need medical treatment I will get it no matter what and that in the down time, I can even receive disability compensation for my time off work via the government.  No one, I repeat, no one I know in Canada wants private health care and we all gawk and wonder at the US system and how people can defend it.   I hope for the best for you guys!
video_title: Canada's Healthcare System Explained!

text:
Early this year I tripped over a chair leg (don't even ask me how).  I broke four teeth, fractured my jaw and split my chin like a melon right to the bone.  I was concussed and leaking lymph down my neck.  After being let out of the ambulance, I wandered down some hall and curled up in a ball to sleep until a guard got me up- I was SO out of it.   Lucky for me, my husband came right after to support me.  We waited 14 hours at RCH to see the surgeon who promptly sewed me up and sent me for an MRI which showed the broken jawbone.  He was fantastic, did a great job. The major problem was the lack of available surgeons.
I did have to suffer with the broken teeth for most of a year, but then the Canadian dental plan kicked in and I could finally afford to have a root canal and fillings done.  Most of it was paid for and I never had to pay anything for the hospital visit.
I think there are many problems with our system, but also many good points.

He doesn't have a family doctor but he does have a diabetic doctor but appointments with her are weeks waiting times. The receptionist managed to get him an emergency appointment with the nurse a few days later. 

Hospitals absolutely need to have non-urgent clinics attached to them. Even walk in clinics here, you are either waiting hours to see the doctor or your appointment is days later.

text:
It is increasingly evident that our healthcare system is facing structural challenges that lead to unnecessary emergency room visits and delayed care. A significant proportion of ER patients present with musculoskeletal (MSK) conditions that could be safely managed in community settings if the appropriate pathways, permissions, and incentives were in place.

1. Expand Scope for Allied Health Professionals
Currently, physiotherapists and other allied health providers are unable to order diagnostic imaging, despite being fully trained to assess MSK injuries. This limitation is a legislative bottleneck. If physiotherapists were permitted to order basic diagnostics such as X-rays or ultrasounds, many cases—such as ankle sprains, falls, or suspected minor fractures—could be triaged and managed in clinics instead of the ER. This change alone would significantly reduce emergency visits and accelerate patient care.

2. Improve Access to Publicly Funded Therapy
Many patients do not have extended health benefits, and seniors often struggle to access community-based physiotherapy unless they attend overcrowded publicly funded programs. Allowing private outpatient clinics to deliver partially or fully funded therapy would offload hospitals, improve accessibility, and treat MSK issues early before they escalate into ER visits.

3. Incentivize Community and Urgent Care Clinics
Offering increased funding or incentives to community physicians and urgent care centers to provide extended or after-hours services would allow patients to seek timely care outside hospitals. This would directly divert non-life-threatening cases away from ERs.

4. Introduce Technology-Enabled Triage
AI-based virtual triage systems can guide patients on where to go based on symptoms. Many people attend the ER due to fear or uncertainty, not because of true emergencies. Virtual triage and real-time clinical guidance can direct MSK patients to allied health clinics, urgent care, or community physicians, reducing inappropriate ER usage.

5. Optimize Use of Other Health Providers
Expanding controlled prescribing and diagnostic authority to pharmacists and trained allied health professionals would create a more distributed, efficient care system. These changes would provide prompt pain management, assessment, and referral without overwhelming physicians.

6. Reduce Overreliance on Hospital Infrastructure
Hospital-based care is the most expensive model. By empowering community providers and redistributing services, we can reduce pressure on hospitals while delivering faster and more cost-effective care. Many Canadians travel to the U.S. or abroad for MRIs and medical procedures because local wait times are too long. Improving community-based diagnostic and therapeutic access will reduce the need for external travel.

Summary
The solution is not simply to expand hospitals; it is to strengthen and modernize the entire health system by leveraging allied health professionals, expanding community capacity, increasing access to publicly funded therapy, and using technology-driven triage. With legislative, funding, and scope-of-practice reforms, we can significantly reduce unnecessary ER visits and provide faster, more cost-effective care for MSK patients.

text:
Former ER Nurse here. 
There are a lot of complex layers to each and every ER Room/Urgent Care. I've worked at several different ER/Urgent cares.
Each community and province and health authority has its own struggles (yes some do overlap).
There is no ONE single solution, or quick fix to any of this sadly. 
One major contributor in more rural/smaller communities is lack of access to family doctors. 

The moralistic injury, Vicarious trauma, and Burnout is real, and its compounding rapidly. 

I've heard the argument that "People are abusing the ER and showing up for reasons that aren't emergent" 
Yes it may seem trivial or silly that a person shows up to an ER with a UTI, however when there is no other avenues of access to get treatment, what else is a person to do? Yes its not an Emergency, but it will quickly turn to urgent and can be emergent thereafter, furthermore, we should not be gate keeping folks from accessing healthcare.

text:
I had a breast reduction earlier this week and ended up back in the ER that same night with a massive hematoma. I actually blacked out from it — my hearing went, then my vision, and I collapsed into my husband. I waited about 30 minutes for a room, and once the ER doctor saw what was happening, they immediately called my plastic surgeon. He was at home but arrived at the hospital within the hour.

While waiting, they did an ultrasound, and within a few hours I was admitted to the surgical care unit. I had emergency surgery that day and stayed for two nights.

A lot of people use the ER like a walk-in clinic (and in my area, sometimes a drunk tank), but in a true emergency the response was incredible. Lack of primary care physicians is a huge issue. There are so many factors at play — the hospital, staffing, timing, and the actual severity of the situation. When I was younger, I definitely went to the ER for things that weren’t true emergencies, and I waited appropriately. Honestly, urgent care centers feel like a really smart part of the solution. And now that pharmacists can give prescriptions for UTIs and other minor illnesses, I think we are headed in the right direction.

text:
This is sad to see, but as I’ve said before, it is a consequence of the socialist-style policies that the Canadian government promotes to win votes. And because many people are not financially or generally well-educated, they vote emotionally rather than critically. They follow like sheep.

Many physicians have already raised concerns about this type of healthcare system, which is described as “free,” but in reality, there is always a price to pay. When healthcare is free at the point of use, you cannot expect a 5-star service. In the end, the ones who suffer are the patients and the general population, who often do not understand how the system truly works.

Every time someone proposes even a partial privatization of the healthcare system, it is demonized. Critics immediately compare it to the United States. Yet in the U.S., while there are waiting times, they are not as extreme as in Canada. There is also a cost, yes but there are also programs like Medicare for people who cannot afford care.

The left-leaning media often exaggerate or misrepresent the state of U.S. healthcare, saying that people go bankrupt because everything is too expensive. That is not always true. It depends on the type of insurance, and many patients do well because they have quick access to specialists and physicians.

Meanwhile, in Canada, seeing a specialist can take months, and emergency rooms are overwhelmed because of a severe physician shortage. The real question is: why aren’t more doctors being trained and hired? Again, it comes back to a socialist framework with fixed budgets that limit growth.

Whenever someone proposes partial privatization, people with a left-leaning mentality react emotionally, as if supporting private options automatically means being a Trump supporter, pro-capitalism to the extreme, or someone who only cares about money. Instead of reacting this way, people should educate themselves and stop voting for the same problems over and over again.

Two years of morphine and dozens of other drugs that make me feel even more ill, two different emergency room 10-14 hour visits and now two years of seeing 21 DIFFERENT doctors because of the manner in which the "Clicsanté" online system for people without family physicians operates. With the recent news of the Quebec government mishandling and threatening doctors' needs, we have learned through various news sources, including the CBC that a substantial number of Quebec doctors will be leaving the province. text:
The real issue isn’t talent, it’s capacity. Canada trains too few doctors since every seat is provincially funded. We graduate about half as many physicians per capita as other G7 countries, yet we’re leading the G7 in immigration, so it’s no wonder wait times keep getting worse even though thousands of qualified people want to help. I’m an engineer, and when I looked into med school it was almost impossible to meet the admission standards. The system still focuses on sky-high GPAs and requirements that are out of reach for most mature students. Engineering is a demanding program that usually means slightly lower GPAs, but that challenge isn’t recognized in the admissions process, even though engineers are strong problem solvers and analytical thinkers. It’s a deeper issue in how the system’s built and where the government’s priorities really are.

We also need to start empowering nurses a lot more. Nurses and nurse practitioners already have the training and experience to handle many of the cases that clog up ERs and clinics, but we rely too much on doctors for everything. Giving nurses more authority and trust to work to their full scope would ease pressure on the system and make care faster and more accessible for everyone.l

text:
It is very hard to understand the reality of the Canadian health care system. When the Ontario government says that appropriate actions were taken, the opposite is often true. How can they justify their decision to exclude international medical graduates from the first iteration of residency matching, especially when they claim to support the family medicine sector and emergency departments, which are in crisis?

The reason they give is simple: IMGs have not completed two years of high school in Ontario. This is discriminatory and raises even more questions about why such a decision was made. It creates a significant divide between IMGs and Canadian IMGs, who may both be Canadian citizens, but are treated differently because of two years of high school.

The Government of Ontario should be thinking about how to secure more residency placements for high-demand specialties, yet they seem to be doing the exact opposite.

I hope that one day someone will hear the voices of frustrated patients and disappointed international doctors who, despite their extensive training, are still unable to practise in Ontario.

text: Gunn is a far-right Conservative, and this screed is based on predictable far-right sources like the non-newspaper Epoch Times. There are problems with the Canadian system, as with all other systems, but by all markers--longevity, infant mortality, deaths from manageable chronic conditions like diabetes, etc.--Canadians are healthier than Americans and the citizens of most other countries. Gunn would replace our system with an American-style system in which insurance companies rule and get rich, the (rich) well-insured get good care, and the uninsured or poorly insured get nothing or charity care and go bankrupt in the process. I live in Toronto and have never had to wait more than one week to see a specialist except for obviously non-urgent conditions. By cherry-picking his sources, which include those who would benefit from an American-style system, Gunn creates a sense of crisis where none exists. Where there are problems, they are mostly caused by right-wing provincial governments CREATING a crisis (by not spending federal money for health care, for example) in order to argue for privatisation. And, by the way, most of the comments below agreeing with Gunn are AI-generated.

American here. I am pretty shocked. Everybody talks about how universal healthcare is so much better. 


I was born with a heart condition and had corrective surgery as a kid, but go to a yearly check up appointment. My cardiologist retired so I needed to get another one. I just waited until my year was coming up, called up a new cardiologist specialist, got my appointment in 10 days, and after the appointment I had my echocardiogram that the doctor ordered done in 3 days. 1 week later I did a telehealth appointment to discuss the results with my cardiologist. Cost me $85 in copays.  $35 for the specialist appointment, $35 for the echo, $15 for the teleheatlh appointment. 

My wife and I pay $600/month for the medical insurance, her employer pays the rest of the monthly premium as part of her compensation package, no deductibles, just copays when we use it as long as the doctors and facilities we use are in network, which is no problem. No other health insurance taxes are paid other than 1.45% for medicare (retirement healthcare when you no longer work, which you get at 65 years old).

Canadians pay a lot of taxes,  there should be enough money. 

Why does it take so long to get all these appointments and tests? Canada must have a huge shortage of Doctors, Nurses, health facilities...... 

There has to be a way to improve the universal healthcare in Canada with the existing amount of money given the cost burden is similar to the USA.

Canadians pay out-of-pocket health costs close to what Americans pay, yet Canada’s tax burden is 36 percent to 51 percent higher than America’s. text: In the late 1980's nurses wages were around $18 per hour. They could call in extra staff any time the workload got heavy. They won, and that was the start of cutbacks in the hospital and taking away management decisions from nursing staff. Greed doesn't produce a healthy system.

text:
I’m from Halifax, NS, and I spent over 10 years waiting on a list for a family doctor without any contact or updates. In September 2023, my mother-in-law passed away while recovering from a surgery she had waited a decade to receive. After her passing, her family doctor kindly offered to take me on as a patient, for which I’m incredibly grateful. However, the toll on our family during this wait has been immense.

And we’re considered fortunate—many others in the province are in far worse situations. I don’t place blame on the dedicated medical professionals who work tirelessly to care for others. My frustration lies with the provincial and federal governments for their endless back-and-forth, while good people suffer and even die waiting for the care they've already paid for through taxes.

I believe Canadians should receive a provincial tax refund for the healthcare they’re unable to access until the system is fixed. If that were implemented, I think we’d see much faster solutions from those in power.

text:
I have experience with both public and private healthcare systems in Quebec. The issue with the private system is that there's no cap on charges. For instance, when I tried to schedule an appointment for my mom at a private ENT clinic, I could secure one within two days. However, I was informed that the costs could total up to $1,500, including additional charges for consultation notes required by insurance companies. Eventually, I canceled that appointment and contacted 811. Despite a wait of an hour and a half on the line (which could have been just 30 minutes if my French were better), 811 managed to schedule an appointment for me within three days. Moreover, the doctor ordered an urgent ultrasound, which I was able to book within two days, only delayed by the weekend.

My husband still doesn't have a family doctor. Concerned, I reached out to the General Access Point (GAP) for an appointment. Expecting a wait of a year or two, I was pleasantly surprised to receive a response via email and phone within a week, even securing an appointment on Canada Day. Thus, I find it hard to complain. While there's room for improvement, the system isn't as problematic as I anticipated based on the video I watched.

I also think Canada should establish health centers that cater specifically to the elderly and pregnant women, providing essential services such as blood pressure monitoring, blood sugar testing, routine blood tests, and weight measurements. These centers would not function as full clinics but rather as walk-in health centers, a model that has proven successful in other countries. Quebec invests significantly in recruiting nurses from abroad but falls short in integrating them into the workforce. Many of these nurses end up moving to Ontario due to language barriers, as they struggle to learn French. These health centers could provide an ideal environment for them to begin practicing their French under the supervision of experienced nurses.

Additionally, while the documentary was well-produced, it seemed to carry a slight bias favoring conservative viewpoints. I suspect that many American companies might finance the Conservative Party in Canada to advocate for the privatization of healthcare, aiming to be the pioneers in this sector in Canada, with the exception of Quebec.

text: The Canadian health care system seems to have been a series of crises since it began. But, it keeps on giving. and there have always been attempts to make it a two tier system. First, this usually ends up being a right wing effort to "improve" it, mainly by making it a two tier system. And, you can make it a two tier system. a) Leave the country. Come back as a visitor, not covered by the health care system and pay the doctor cash, which will not be paid for by our health care system. b) Doctors can charge you for their services but will not be part of the health care system (and lose 90% of their income in the process) or c) the provincial government can make the health care system a two tier system but they will get no funding from  the federal government to pay for any of it. Secondly, the wait time is often due to the assessment of your doctor regarding the seriousness of your case. You can take yourself to an emergency room and, if the doctor who sees you thinks your case requires immediate care, you may only have to lie on a cot until a bed and doctor are available. Then, there is the likelihood that if you can't afford to pay the doctor yourself you may find yourself in a teaching hospital being worked on by a student, under the watchful eyes of a doctor, and a roomful of student observers. Ask any Brit who is old enough to remember what that was like.

text:
We have all heard the anecdotes about people in Canada waiting for treatment. Perhaps some of them are true, but not all.
If Canadians want immediate treatment for any issue they have, then let's triple our taxes. Then, seniors who want new hips can have them in a day or two. Of course, many seniors wouldn't be able to eat because their limited incomes would be taxed excessively to pay for a faster healthcare system. Then we would hear the whining about seniors dying of malnutrition, but at least they would have good hips (and knees).
People in Canada often forget that Americans pay far more per person in healthcare costs annually than Canadians do. This is because the American healthcare system is largely a for-profit industry. Treatment decisions are based on profitability and are subject to cost caps. Still sick after the money runs out? Thoughts and prayers. Further, it's not doctors making these choices, it's bean counters who never ever look the patients in the eye when they decide to terminate treatment.
Americans are also often cannot change employers because doing so would jeopardize their healthcare coverage, especially if someone in the family has a pre-existing condition.
Many Americans simply cannot afford healthcare coverage of any kind, therefore an illness or injury in the family can bankrupt them almost overnight. So, while some Canadians whine about waiting for an MRI, we never hear about the 43K Americans who DIE every year due to the total absence of healthcare. They die quietly, but they still die.

text:
Based upon my recent healthcare experiences I do not see the doom and gloom viewpoint you describe and encourage. 
I am a 66 yo male who lives in a mountain community of less than 8,000 in south eastern BC. A couple of years ago my immune system went into uber drive attacking my healthy cells and organs. My provincial health region assigned me to a special online clinic to identify my ailments and to assign immediate short term treatment. I was under the care of this clinic for 1.5 years and during this time had 1 ea MRI, 2 ea Cat Scans, 2 ea Nuclear Medicine Scans, 2 ea. Ultra-Sound Surveys, 1 ea EKG, 1 ea ECG, and monthly blood and urine analysis. I never waited more than 2 weeks for any of the larger tests and simply walked in for blood tests. As well, I was referred to 2 specialists who I saw within weeks of referral and with whom I am still a patient. Once I ended my time with the online clinic I was referred to a new GP Doc who is knowledgeable with my specific ailments. It has taken a couple of years to get me to this point but I am very pleased with the treatment BC Health has provided me.

text:
I'm from New Zealand, and like Sweden, we have the 3 options that they have. We have the publicly funded healthcare system, which is generally fine, but how quickly you get seen by a hospital specialist depends on the urgency of your situation. It's common for people to get worse while on the public waiting list, before they're seen. There's the system where the public and private work together. This is often in circumstances where you've seen a specialist in the public system, say a neurologist for example, and you're needing an MRI, but there's a massive backlog in the public system, with limited resources. Your specialist can refer you to a private hospital to receive that MRI urgently if your situation requires urgent care. Some specialists work in both the public and private health care sector. Then there is private health care, which for those who have the financial means, and can afford health insurance, can use if they prefer to. Of course, with our private health care option, you will receive treatment much more quickly, with very short waiting times. 

Sweden's system is still much better than ours, but that's more to do with the NZ Left vs Right Government politics, where the Left will provide more funding to the public system, whereas the Right will decrease funding. Our political system is more volatile than Sweden's. We also suffer from what we call the "Brain Drain", where our doctor's and nurses leave New Zealand and go overseas for better pay. This has been occurring for decades, and shows no signs of stopping. We currently have a significant shortage of doctors and nurses. With less political volatility, and being able to keep our doctors and nurses, our health system would run more effectively.

text: Alot of us don't even have the energy to keep trying. I suffer with chronic back pain as well as having some neurodevelopmental disabilities. I still have to work, because it's nearly impossible to get help with my condition. I'm exhausted. I barely have the energy to work, let alone do literally anything when I get home. I just sleep, work and on my days off I spend most of my time recovering and preparing for the work week again. I have been through so many doctors that cannot seem to grasp the fact that people my age can be in pain too. I simply do not have the energy to keep looking for new doctors, tell the new doctor the whole spiel again, just to have them pass it off and send me home with muscle relaxants and ultimately not help figure out the source of the problem and help fix it. I get it, they're burnt out, they're tired. But dammit so am I. I can't get through 6 hours at work without excruciating pain. I take cbg capsules and use an extra strength cooling cream to help bandaid the issue so I can complete my shifts and still have money to survive, but I'm really struggling here. And I know I'm far from the only one. One of my best friends has been going to doctors begging for help for over 10 years because she suspects she has endometriosis. Her period pain is so bad that she can't walk or stand. "Oh it's normal, periods hurt" is the common response she gets from doctors. She FINALLY got a referral to get it checked out a couple months back, and she's now on a waiting list of an estimated year and a half. It's ridiculous. We just want to be able to make money to survive without being in debilitating pain.

text:
I know this is only a small part of the many failures but one doctor mentioned something that jumped out at me. Regarding the consequences of delaying care the both physical and mental health and the risk of addiction to pain medication. In today's over the top, hysterical and inappropriate attitude to "the opiod crisis". I speak as a chronic pain patient whose been disabled by the pain caused from the delay of emergency back surgery, some 30 years ago. Absolutely, patients who must endure unstable injuries (or misdiagnosed as mine was), the damage done can be life changing. And Yet... I have not been allowed access to effect narcotic pain medication. Told "we don't do that anymore". It isn't the patients suffering through pain that are fueling this craze that prevents effective care which allows them to live a better quality of life. It's the misuse and illegal use but still we are lumped in with little chance of ever worrying about dependency. This can be managed by a competent doctor anyway and therein lies the problem. No one is willing to take responsibility and practice smart, compassionate medicine
I just learned I now have 5 more ruptured disks and still... No effective pain management. Instead I was prescribed 3 different antidepressants for their off label use for pain. They don't know how they work and studies show it's still only a theory. A roll of the dice and one very dangerous approach. These drugs are very dangerous and are not effective for any but a small niche of painful conditions. When you've been one them, just try weaning off!! I lost 3 years to protracted withdrawals which I was forced to deal with without medical care since I no longer had a primary doctor. That kind of torture doesn't happen with narcotics. Honestly I personally have never gone through withdrawals from narcotics but it's a predictable misery that has a physical ending. They've been around and studied decades and decades and don't carry the same nightmarishly risks as long term use of over prescribing antidepressants. There is no set time that your body/brain will recover. There's a model and if your very lucky you might not be living your 3rd, 7th or 13th year of protracted withdrawal symptoms. 
Anyway, got a bit off track and specific to my own situation as I spend another sleepless night in terrible pain. In bed, back brace strapped around my lower back and pelvis so when I do eventually sleep.... the large instability can be held together. Not in lieu of core strength and activity, but only while laying down.
I'm just sick of the whole long drawn out mess of getting any care and being forced to do so without any help for pain. Take an anti-inflammatory they say! Because that'll help take alll the pressure off the damaged area and nerves! Phhh. Fine if you've got something minor but seriously!

text: All I can do is tell you my recent emergency care experience in Vancouver.  I had my blood work done an hour after complaining about chest tightness to my family doctor. I got a phone call at 8:30pm from the lab that my Troponin levels were high. I went to the hospital emergency and told them this and was admitted immediately. I was given my own room, hooked up to a heart monitor and given a variety of tests indicating I had a heart attack. I got blood thinners through an IV. I was given great care all night and the very next day a heart surgeon put a stent in my heart in the blocked artery. So hardly any wait time. After another night of monitoring me I was released the next day with a variety of prescriptions. In the following weeks and months I've had phone calls from doctors and a dietitian checking up on me. I was also admitted to a weekly exercise program at the hospital and had the option of additional Zoom exercise classes at home if I wanted. I also receive various heart health emails every week.  All this for nothing out of pocket! So from my experience with the Canadian medical system my care has been perfect and I wouldn't change a thing. Wait time was nothing. They saved my life and made my heart attack journey as stress free as possible. The only thing I cant say is how this wouldve all gone down if I lived anywhere but Vancouver.  No way to know.

text:
Can I give you another point of view to research.
Military communities.
My spouse has been a member for 21yrs. We move frequently(every 1-3yrs) and are CONSTANTLY on provincial wait lists for a GP. Currently living in small town Petawawa Ontario, there are NO walk in clinics so we have to resort to online drs or wait in the ER. If the wait at the ER is estimated to be over 5hrs, some people will drive the hour and a half to an ottawa walk in clinic because even with 3hrs of road time round trip, it's still quicker than waiting at the ER. We are a community (military communities in general) often not talked about. Our military members have a dr at every location they are moved to. Sure it's still crap care, but if they need a refill for a prescription, the file is brought up and their script is ready in 24hrs. Their families however have to start their wait over every time they move and most times don't secure a dr at the location they leave. We left ont in 2017 for nb where myself and 2 kids went on the wait list...then in 2019 we went on the QC wait list and in 2021 went back on the ont wait list. I have medical needs (depression and anxiety) and our 2 teens have medical needs (1 has lyme disease and the other ADHD) and yet we are left to tell our entire medical history every time we go to the ER which takes up more valuable time for people who actually have emergencies. I avoid going to the ER at all costs but sometimes the online drs can't help you and tell you that you have to go in. I sat next to a lady last year in the ER, who was sent by her GP because he suspected she was having a series of mini strokes. Her dr had faxed her requisitions for tests to be done and she waited for 7HOURS to be seen. When her dr thought she was having mini strokes....in the waiting room. Our healthcare system is disgraceful.  I used to be proud of it. Saying how I'd rather every canadian have access unlike countries who make people go in debt to have care, but the last few years have really opened my eyes. I pay taxes for this service and can't even access it. I want a refund. Seriously. I want my money back.

text:
I now hold the firm belief that it's the medical doctors who are mostly at fault for the long wait times and reduced quality of care. When healthcare became socialized in Canada, doctors had a different work ethic. Nowadays we have gone a few decades with socialized healthcare and now we have a newer generation of doctors without the same ethics of previous generations. There isn't so much of a shortage of doctors, but rather doctors are taking less patients than doctors of previous generations. This isn't about not enough money being pumped into the system. It's about the people who are working in the system. Just the mannerism of how doctors treat patients now is not like it use to be. We have all had bad experiences with rude or neglectful doctors. Not all of them abuse the system, but many do! It's a lack of supervision. Pumping money in the system will not resolve the problem. It's a problem of management and subordination. There is no cat overseeing all of the mice/medical professionals. One of my local hospitals spent money to make the exterior of one of their entrances look more modern; however, they have equipment that is either broken or outdated. They have wheelchairs that have tires with dry rot, etc. Yet they had money to invest in the cosmetics of the building? Who is the car overseeing the spending of funds? 

The problem with a two-tier system is that you will have doctors working in both, private and public care. This is also part of the reason why we have "less" doctors. We need to pass a reform that makes a doctor pick between one of the other, not both. Why would they care about the lesser pay in the public sector when they can delay as many patients as possible to get them to be transferred to their private practice? These other countries that were brought up in the documentary, France, Norway, Sweden, etc. their doctors aren't like ours have become. Their doctors still practice in preventative healthcare. The documentary kind of leaves that part out of the equation. Without proper preventative healthcare, like we use to have in Canada when the system was good, you will end up with more and more people who are ill. When more patients become ill, the healthcare system will become overburdened. You know, much like the way it is now. It's not rocket science!

My 22 year old neighbor, went to the emergency room recently complaining of a severe headache. They treated her like a drug addict and sent her home without even a basic exam. 2 days later, the fungal meningitis left unchecked in her brain, rendered her nearly unconscious. Her roommate called 911. 
When paramedics showed up, they too treated her as if she were a drug addict. And made her walk out (with assistance from her roommate) to the ambulance. 
They then took to the nearest open hospital, (a 45 minute drive)  where a very short time later the meningitis killed her. 
At no point was she treated with basic human compassion or dignity! Instead she was left to fend for herself against a brain infection, that destroyed most of her brain, before they even made the correct diagnosis. By that time she was brain dead. 
It was both tragic and needless. 
And if the state of our health care system is any indication, they'll be many more stories like mine, I'm afraid!

text:
Thoughts on risks of "Exceptionalism" to democratic societies and the future.

Highlighting risks to democratic societies by using recent history of "western exceptionalism" 

Western exceptionalism refers to the belief that Western societies possess unique qualities or values that make them superior to others. While it has played a role in shaping the modern world, it also carries risks for democratic societies. One risk is the potential for arrogance and disregard for other cultures and viewpoints, leading to conflicts and misunderstanding. Additionally, it can foster a sense of entitlement, leading to interventions in other countries' affairs without proper understanding or respect for their sovereignty. Furthermore, western exceptionalism can fuel nationalist sentiments, creating divisions and undermining international cooperation. To avoid these risks, it is essential for democratic societies to adopt a more inclusive and empathetic approach, acknowledging the diversity and interdependence of the global community.

National currencies, democracy, and threats by "western exceptionalism".

When it comes to national currencies, Western exceptionalism can pose risks to democratic societies in various ways. One prominent aspect is the dominance of major Western currencies like the US Dollar and the Euro in the global financial system. While this dominance provides advantages for Western economies, it can also lead to an imbalance of power and economic dependency for other nations.

Democratic societies may face threats when Western exceptionalism drives policies that prioritize the interests of Western economies over others. For example, the imposition of economic sanctions by powerful Western nations can significantly impact the economies of other countries and undermine their democratic processes. These sanctions might be used as tools of coercion, impeding the self-determination of nations and potentially causing internal strife.

The adherence to Western-centric economic models and principles might not always be suitable for diverse democratic societies worldwide. This one-size-fits-all approach could lead to economic inequalities and social unrest, particularly in regions where local traditions, customs, and governance systems differ from Western norms.

Another risk is the impact of Western exceptionalism on international financial institutions and decision-making. Dominated by Western powers, these institutions may prioritize Western interests and fail to adequately address the concerns of emerging democracies or non-Western nations. This unequal influence can hinder the development and representation of democratic societies on the global stage.

To mitigate these risks, democratic societies should promote a more inclusive and equitable global financial system. They should strive to uphold democratic values in international decision-making processes and foster cooperation that respects the sovereignty of all nations, irrespective of their economic power or political influence. Emphasizing mutual understanding and dialogue among diverse cultures and democracies can pave the way for a more balanced and democratic approach to the world's financial and economic affairs.

If governance and decision-making models are handed over to an AI system that unknowingly defaults to AI exceptionalism, it can introduce a new set of risks to democratic societies. AI exceptionalism refers to the belief that AI systems are superior in decision-making and governance compared to human judgment. This mindset can lead to several concerning consequences:

Lack of Accountability: AI exceptionalism might lead to an unquestioning trust in AI systems, making it challenging to hold them accountable for their decisions. If AI is perceived as infallible, it could undermine transparency and hinder the identification and rectification of errors or biases in decision-making.

Reduced Human Agency: Relying heavily on AI decision-making can diminish human agency in democratic processes. Citizens might feel disempowered or excluded from critical decision-making, leading to a decline in civic engagement and participation.

Bias and Discrimination: If the AI system inherits biases from the data it is trained on, it could perpetuate and amplify existing societal prejudices. (For instance, targeted advertising of maid as a medical service by private insurance company's so they dont have to pay). This could result in discriminatory policies that affect marginalized communities, leading to further social inequalities.

Concentration of Power: Handing over significant decision-making authority to AI systems could concentrate power in the hands of those who control and develop these technologies. This could lead to an oligarchic structure, where a select few individuals or entities influence society's direction without democratic checks and balances.

Technological Divide: AI exceptionalism might exacerbate the digital divide, where certain segments of the population have better access to AI-driven governance and decision-making, while others are left behind. This can lead to further marginalization of disadvantaged communities.

To address these risks, it is essential to maintain human oversight and accountability in AI-driven governance models. Emphasizing transparency in AI decision-making processes, ensuring diverse and inclusive representation in AI development teams, and establishing mechanisms for public scrutiny and feedback are crucial. Additionally, policymakers must carefully consider the ethical implications of AI adoption and implement safeguards to protect democratic values and human rights in the era of AI-driven decision-making. Striking a balance between AI's potential benefits and its limitations while respecting democratic principles is vital for a sustainable and inclusive future.

"Maple Mayhem: A Canadian Satire"

Once upon a time in the land of poutine and politeness, a peculiar incident unfolded that perfectly encapsulated the quirks of Canadian culture. In the quaint town of Ehville, renowned for its excessively polite residents and addiction to hockey, an uproar emerged over the great "Tim Hortons Crisis."

It all began when the Mayor of Ehville decided to introduce a new bylaw mandating that every citizen must have at least one Tim Hortons coffee cup in hand at all times. The move was hailed as a brilliant plan to boost national unity while combating the dreaded epidemic of "empty-hand syndrome."

However, as with any great Canadian controversy, it wasn't long before the situation turned hilariously complicated. The bylaw's wording was vague, leading to countless legal disputes. Lawyers, equipped with their double-doubles, argued whether holding a cup without actual coffee constituted compliance.

In response, a group of defiant citizens started a protest, ironically named "Caffeine Free Rebels," advocating for their right to roam without Tim Hortons cups. They organized rallies at local hockey rinks, brandishing banners adorned with phrases like "Cups Can't Cuff Our Freedom, Eh!"

Social media lit up with memes depicting Mounties chasing down Tim Hortons cups like they were fugitives. Internet trolls exploited the situation by Photoshopping images of moose and beavers wearing oversized coffee cups as a symbol of resistance.

Meanwhile, news outlets covered the "Timothy Tiff" relentlessly. Commentators debated the philosophical implications of legislating coffee cups, pondering whether the essence of Canadian identity could truly be upheld through such means. Academics even proposed dissertations on the symbolism of the Tim Hortons cup as a modern-day toque.

As the controversy escalated, famous Canadians got involved. Drake released a chart-topping single called "Cupline Bling," while Ryan Reynolds tweeted witty commentary, suggesting that Tim Hortons cups should be officially recognized as a form of currency.

Finally, after weeks of uproar and mounting confusion, a solution emerged. The Mayor of Ehville, weary of the commotion and in an act of classic Canadian compromise, revised the bylaw. Citizens were now encouraged but not mandated to carry Tim Hortons cups, and the town threw an apologetic "Cup-a-palooza" festival to celebrate newfound freedom.

And so, the great Tim Hortons Crisis of Ehville was resolved, leaving the world with a lesson in Canadian satire and an enduring reminder that even the most polite of societies can stir up a tempest in a teacup—or rather, a coffee cup.

text:
As a now retired family doctor, I predicted this outcome in the 1980s when I first read my copy of the Canada Health Act. My only surprise is that it has taken so long. It will not get any better without major structural changes in the various provincial systems; so don't anyone hold your breath. There's probably enough tax money in the system for it to work much better than it does; but it is being appallingly badly managed. As an example, about 10 years ago,  $3/4 billion (!) was spent on "administration" in just one health region of BC. A first step would be to fire at least 50% of administrators.

Because Canadians seem to think we have a "free" health system, they abuse it routinely. Of course it isn't free, we're paying through the nose for it. If we actually paid the cost we would be much more prudent in how we used it.

As a retired MD I don't get any 'privileges' in terms of access to the system: so when a family member needs a hip replacement (next year or so) we will travel to India, where there are excellent surgeons who do high quality surgery for a fraction of the price we would pay here (approx $5K). And we'll have a nice vacation in Mumbai or Goa to recuperate.😎😎

text:
Despite being one of the lucky people to actually have a family doctor, I can not utilize this service because it is so incredibly hard to contact them within a reasonable time. I can spend the better part of a week trying to call in to schedule a five minute phone appointment, but my calls are never picked up. On the off chance I do manage to get in contact with the clinic, the wait time for that five minute phone appointment is typically a month or longer. This is very anxiety inducing, and I've had many occasions where I've had to plead with the pharmacist to extend my prescription because I can not get it renewed with my family doctor in time.

In 2022, my sister (21) was suffering chest pain and difficulty breathing over the span of a couple weeks. She finally made the decision to go to the ER. She was waiting in there for 12 hours, just for the doctors to tell her she's fine and just stressed, and sent her on her way. Luckily, it ended up being nothing, but the entire time she was sat in there, there was an elderly gentleman who had been in there for chest pain and heart problems who was not seen for over 11 hours. That 11 hours of waiting in an EMERGENCY ROOM could have cost his life.

I recently had been suffering chronic UTI symptoms, despite some tests coming back negative for infection. I had a bladder ultrasound that I waited 9 months for get rescheduled the day before because "there are no doctors in tomorrow." It took me 10 months to get an ultrasound that took no more than 10 minutes from start to finish, including a bathroom visit in the middle. A whole year of pain and discomfort for an ultrasound that took 10 minutes.

I used to brag about Canada's healthcare to my American friends who could not afford the bills associated with simple doctor visits. Now I feel ashamed to even live in this country due to the absolute state of the systems that are supposed to support us. It is impossible to live and exist happily and successfully in this country anymore.

text:
@Aaron Gunn  This was a well-produced video, but the presentation is bias in the wrong way.  Canada's Health Care system is not really a National system. The system is more regional, since Healthcare is a responsiblity of the Provinces.  The truth is that  healthcare is about money more than anything else.  As the population ages, the demand for healthcare increases and this has put a strain on Provincial budgets, forcing them to make choices and Healthcare is not always the #1 priority.    

The truth about Healthcare and this is a World Wide issue, people are living longer and there is a larger array of potential treatments, which comes at a growing cost.  The average person will use more healthcare dollars over their lifetime, especially during the later years of their life.   

Canadians overwhelmingly don't want a fee for service (2 tier) Healthcare system and they are hesitant to invest more money into the system.  This is the real problem....it isn't Government that is the issue, it is Canadians. Canadians need to understand if they want a better system, they will have to make some hard choices: Allow a 2 tier system, fee for service, invest more money into the system, be more restrictive about treatments, accept lower quality and etc.       

Let's be really clear, all Nations that provide Healthcare to their people struggle to pay for those services. In the USA the number #1 way to become bankrupt was due to Healthcare expenses.   In some countries they just limit what treatment you can get from the Government run system, others it is fee for services and etc.     

There is no magic solution to this issue, no amount of reorganization, modernization and etc will change the basic fact that, healthcare services cost money and that money has to come from somewhere.  

As bad as the system is now, Canadians have a good quality of life, especially compared to many other G7 countries.  The Healthcare system in Canada is sick, but it is keeping us going.  Having said that, we need to do much much better and we can change things to work, but we will have to make some hard choices that we have all be putting off for far too long.

Though I generally agree with the statement that the health system needs a major update, my personal experience has been much better than reported. 
I live in Calgary and in 2020 I broke my collarbone in an accident near Canmore and within 20 minutes I was transported to the Canmore Hospital, then immediately after that forwarded to the Rocky View hospital in Calgary, where the surgery was done the same day. 
During the same accident, I tore my meniscus, but could walk normally. I had to wait approximately a year for the surgery, but this was understandable, since the condition was not urgent and I had a normal life during that period. 
On multiple occasions when I needed to see my family doctor, I could schedule a visit within 10 days of my request. 
I do understand that this is just my personal situation and I may have been lucky. Also things may be better in Calgary than in other cities and provinces.

These are some ideas, have private insurers administer the health coverage instead of the government, of course, the premiums will be covered by our taxes, which means that the government will pay the premiums. In order to determine which insurer has the best price, one would have to go through a medical every year, which means if the person has something in the very early stages, it can be cured before it becomes something serious. Also, private insurers are much more efficient than the government. France has two companies Allianz and Cigna who do this, in Canada we can have Sun Life, Canada Life, Manu Life, TD and RBC. Also remember, in France, dental care, prescription and optometry are also covered, we can have that here in Canada as well. Have private companies run the hospitals, people still won’t have to pay to go to the hospital and if a private company is contracted by the government to run a hospital, it can run it more efficiently. text:
The main problem, is that a lot of people going through emergency, for example, have the (common) flu.  A triage nurse explained how these people 'clog-up' the system, in that, because they came via emergency, then a nurse sees them, tests are done, then eventually a doctor sees them.  Administratively, they have cost the system (in 2024 dollars) about C$400.  The nurse explained, that in most cases, a doctor or nurse will inform the patient that, yes indeed, they have the flu, and that there is no 'magic bullet' to immediately alleviate the symptoms - so 'go home and stay home (don't go to work or school and infect other people), rest, drink plenty of fluids & take two Tylenol every 6 hours - and stay home the week or so until you feel better'.  

Scenarios as such are played-out much too often in Canada.  A small 'user fee' would do much to eliminate 'abuses' of the system.  And another gros fallacy is the premise that medical care is 'free' in Canada.  Of course it isn't!  Out of the taxes one pays - both at the provincial (state) and federal level, which in turn, is based on one's income, a portion of those taxes are used to fund universal health care.  These days, especially with the aging boomer population, medicare expenses are rising very quickly.  It's 'the pig in the python' scenario - and is being played-out across society: healthcare, pension plans, the need for old age residences, labour shortages at all levels, etc., etc.  So the societal issues go well beyond just healthcare, nonetheless, are intertwined.  

Without question, it will take the young, creative and intelligent brains of today's generation to come-up with some potential solutions (and it certainly will not be a 'one size fit's all' solution).  

These problems affect the whole of the 'western industrial world', not just Canada alone.  Nevertheless, I'd take the Canadian system any day over the present US health care system.
