text:
The currency conversions in this presentation are flawed. It is wrong to convert Canadian dollars to U.S. dollars. Why? We do not spend U.S. dollars in Canada to acquire goods and services. We spend Canadian dollars.

Remember too, the Canadian dollars consistently trades at a 25% discount on the U.S. dollar. i.e. $1 Canadian = $1.25 U.S.

So the presentation should have said, for example, Canadians spend $100 (CAD) while Americans spend $118 (USD) on medical services. That is a more accurate comparison.

One other point that goes unmentioned is despite Canada's universal healthcare system being funded by taxes, Canada has among the lowest overall tax burdens of the OECD countries, lower than the U.S.

By the way, the name of the largest country in the western hemisphere Cah na da, not Key na da.

text: Healthcare has three gradable factors: Affordability, Availability and Quality. No country has all three. Canada has decent quality, it’s obviously affordable, but the availability is terrible. Average wait times are around 10-weeks to see a specialist, 11-weeks to get something like an MRI, and 3.5-weeks for an ultrasound. Also 1/4 patients have to wait up to 25-weeks to see a specialist. The US has the best quality, the best accessibility/wait times and 92% of Americans are covered by health insurance. Average wait time is 3x faster than Canada for specialists and there are urgent cares/ERs in almost every single town in the US. Don’t get me wrong, there’s a ton to improve on. But if someone were to chose where to get a surgery they’d chose the US, and the availability is a no contest. So what it comes down to is cost — which improvements can be made. But what we can’t do is continue to advocate for the idea that anyone who touches US soil gets free healthcare. Canada doesn’t do that and their system is actually free.

text: Being a Canadian cancer survivor, and both my parents having had cancer, our treatments in the USA would have bankrupted us. We would have lost our home, our cars, and my parents' business. Our wait time for elective surgeries need improvement, but medically necessary treatments are done immediately. Canada is a tax-heavy jurisdiction and we pay for this via our taxes, which may not seem very sellable at first glance. Also, healthcare is administered by the provinces and territories, not the federal government. Our costs however are high in terms of provincial budgets. My province spent 40% of its budget on health care last year, and it's difficult to reign this in. Also, family doctors essentially are private contractors who bill their respective provinces for service, so it's not like physicians are government operatives. Also, most drugs are not covered - many of us have drug plans through our work that help us along with that as well as for services like dentistry or optometry. It's not a perfect system, but it saved my life and I'm grateful for that. Also, a system that works for a country of 38,000,000 may not work as well for a country of 330,000,000. But Americans need to decide that for themselves. In conclusion: not a perfect system, and it needs reform and improvement. But in my opinion, vastly better overall in terms of outcomes and resulting financial difficulty than the US system.

text:
In Canada, if you need urgent care, you will get it. That is my experience. But, Canadian health care is not a "health care on demand" system.  
People in gate-keeper positions like the triage nurses in an emergency ward of a hospital decide who gets the next available physician.  So yes, it is not unusual to wait for an hour to get attended to to if you have suffered a sprain in a fall for example, while other people are brought in by ambulance after your arrival, suffering major traumas from a traffic accident.  So yes there are wait times, but they are not unreasonable ones.  Ditto  for referrals from your GP to a specialist - you might wait 7 weeks if your situation is not too serious or painful.  Provincial ministries of health measure this data and manage capital spending and capacity in the system. Ultimately, it is Canadian voters' choice whether it is better to spend more on health care or less.
Look at the performance of the Canadian health care system during the pandemic. Overall, pretty good. Better in some provinces than others. The weakest zone is long-term care for the elderly where transmission rates were frequently elevated, and there are now plans to improve that.  Compare this outcome with USA and other countries. 
If anything, as a Canadian, I would like system coverage, with annual limits, expanded to include certain licensed professionals such as psychologists, naturopaths, traditional Chinese medicine practitioners, dentists.
video_title: How Canada's Universal Health-Care System Works

text:
I've lived in Canada for most of my life. This review glosses over the *biggest problem* with Canadian Health Care. 

*It's not Evidence-Based*

In the USA, to my knowledge, you do the testing first and then the doctor interprets the testing. In Canada you don't even get to have testing done unless a doctor AGREES you need it(irregardless of whether you pay for it or not).
No doctor's approval, *no testing* and believe me there are plenty of doctors too stingy to get their patients properly tested.
There is also no way to get any kind of blood work testing done without a doctor's referral either.

If you consider how much more we pay in Income and Sales/Property taxes in Canada, and then factor in the price of private insurance ANYWHERE *except the United States where networks have created a monopoly system*; Canadians pay more for substandard care. I can cover myself and my wife 100% FULL COVERAGE without a deductible *WORLD WIDE* (except USA) through Regency for Expats for just $5000USD per year. 

Heck I was able to get a full suite of diagnostic tests including bloodwork, xray, cardiogram and ultrasound in Indonesia for $500. Here I couldn't even get a full bloodwork done for my wife who needed the result for her Dietician to formulate on.

Depending on where you live the wait times for the hospitals can be absurd, I remember waiting 6-8 hours to get seen by a doctor at a local hospital when I was younger. Not only do you have to wait, but they *MAKE YOU WAIT IN THE SAME AREA AS ALL THE SICK PEOPLE WHO HAVE FLU/LORD KNOWS WHAT*.

Many Canadians actually go to private clinics, where the wait times are a bit more manageable, ~2- 3 hours but still absurd.

I once went to Hospital in Shenzhen China, the wait time *wasn't even an hour*, and the total cost of the visit and the medication was less than *$50*.

text: My understanding of the Canadian healthcare system is that triage is a core element.  One having a heart attack is seen immediately, one with a sore foot will wait, one going to the ER will be seen in due course depending on wait times and severity of the issue.  If one schedules an appointment with a specialist, the wait time can be long assuming the issue is not an emergency.  Overall this CNBC review seems to be a fair snapshot.  One thing that is only touched upon is how payment is made into the system.  The typical Canadian pays for their healthcare Insurance through taxation, while the typical American pays for their healthcare through direct payment, private insurance, or as part of their company plan if fortunate enough to have this option.  While Canadian individual taxes are higher in Canada, the offset of Americans paying for insurance on top of taxes is more (per my relatives in the US).

text: I am a Canadian and a few years ago my wife became very ill during a trip we took to San Francisco. She had to be taken to the hospital to be treated there. It was weird goin the the emergency room there on a typical day. The first thing they asked was "How are you paying for this?". The doctor and emergency room was hidden until you passed the cashier. Fortunately we purchased travel insurance before our trip. Once we got our paperwork was done we got to go into the emergence room. It was EMPTY. Like no patients. That was very eerie to us. Obviously we got to see a doctor right away. He checked her over did some blood work and a CT scan because she had passed out in the hotel room prior to going to the hospital. She was given an IV to increase her fluid level and we were sent back to the hotel. The total cost for a 2 hour visit was $11,000. That's when it hit me that many people are afraid to go to the hospital because of the cost. I asked a nurse about it and she said that even those with insurance are afraid as they are not sure that their insurance will approve and cover the costs. The next day we headed home with my wife still very ill. Once we landed I took her straight to the hospital. Yep back in Canada and to a full emergency waiting room. She was triaged by a nurse and were given a bed right away skipping many of the people who have been there a long time. In Canada we are not treated by first come first serve but by the severity of your need. After some initial assessment she was transferred to a bed in the hospital for further diagnosis. By this time she was extremely ill. Her only symptom was sever diarrhea which would happen 10-15 times a day. She was constantly dehydrated and very low on potassium despite being given plenty of IV fluids with potassium. After a battery of tests and scans it was determined that she had a rare pancreatic cancer called VIPoma. She went into surgery and had the tumor removed. Her total time in the hospital was 2 months and it cost us $0 dollars. So even though the Canadian system is flawed I can say that I will take it over the American system just based on cost alone.

text: Comment about long wait times, as I live in Canada. Three major driving forces as I see it. Population grows faster than hospital capacity grows. My city is booming, even Schools are overflowing. Government doesn't keep up in building capacity (previous Provincial government had a recommendation to add more capacity for nearly a decade but didn't hence we voted them out). In general, Government looks at changes in the last census to decide on what and where to build more capacity. But many cities grow so fast, they need to look annually for what they do build is already overflowing. Finally, more seniors and more seniors as the population ages. We're living longer and this also impacts the health care system. Now that is the big cities. In rural areas, different story, long wait times are typically lack of doctors. They just will not stay (move back to the big city) and in some places there is nothing but a long ambulance ride to the nearest ER and hope you make it. Not so much the system, its the lack in keeping up with the demand in urban and rural centres.

text:
I WORKED in a huge trauma center... 
The LONG wait lines are for TWO type of people.. 
A) People that come to the hospital Emergency with a common cold or a sprained finger, etc... Every new arrival sees a TRIAGE nurse that takes their vitals and finds out the reason and applies a PRIORITY Status (usually P1 to P5) ... You can imagine that a LOT of people go to the hospital for little things, because its FREE!  The get mad about the delay, we EXPLAIN the reason and they just don't want to get it.. 
B) We are a small country, Doctors study what they want and some specialties are not very appealing to a lot of medical students. That crates a rarity in some sectors. In Quebec, dermatologists and some other specialties are getting little to no replacements. THAT means long waiting times for some patients. 
PERSONALLY! I have needed my Health Services a LOT! I have had MANY major surgeries, accidents and other illnesses. I have NEVER waited TOO long... 
You HAVE to understand, In Canada MONEY won't let you go to the front of the line.. 
We would NEVER let insurance companies hold our lives in their FOR PROFIT world...

Our private medical Insurance is for Ambulance, Dental and better hospital beds.

As for DRUGS, I pay a MAXIMUM TOTAL of 90$ a month or 1000$ a year.. NO MATTER how much the total is.. I live in Quebec...
And YES, we want it better, but its STILL a lot better than the US system of RICH and INSURED ONLY!
And YEAH! We like to shove it to people that think they are better than everyone else sometimes..

They conveniently forgot to mention how high our TAXES are to pay for our healthcare. 

Anyone making serious $$$ pays 40-50% income tax, plus 15% tax on virtually all goods and services (yes, we pay GST on every service visit, every trades person, every dental visit, every product we buy with groceries exempt) plus tax on gas and property tax. So yes, we love our health care, but make no mistake, we PAY for it.

text:
The problem with publicly funded universal healthcare is that, although it will, in theory, work for the entire national population, in practice it will favour the privately insured and anyone without private insurance will die in the queue before any treatment. A good alternative would be the Swiss system: there is no public healthcare system funded by the government, but yet it is universal because everyone living in Switzerland is required to have a contract with health insurance. There are countless health care insurance companies, from which you have a free choice and which are also under governmental regulation. This would avoid preferential treatment for privately insured persons as anyone is insured the same way, and there would be an open competition, which would satisfy the opponents of state intervention. It certainly has its downsides as well, but internationally it is considered as one of the best!

text:
The vast majority of hospitals in Canada are owned and operated by not-for-profit or governmental health organizations that must follow the Government's rules --- technically, that makes them "private" but essentially, they are "public" because they get their funding from Government and must follow Government healthcare regulations.   Doctors own and operate their own offices or clinics to treat and diagnose appointment patients or walk-ins but these are not hospitals which are essentially "public" institutions.  The doctors who diagnose/treat patients in their own offices/clinics then bill the government.

It's true Canada's healthcare system needs to improve in a variety of ways --- more preventative programs,  more funding for eldercare and publicly subsidized seniors' care homes, expanded coverage to include prescription drugs, eyecare, dental care, etc.   All of this became clearer with the pandemic.  Still,  I wouldn't trade Canada's healthcare with the greedy system in the U.S. that thinks it's perfectly fine to take profits off sick people and ignore those many who can't afford healthcare.  Our taxes are high but I'd rather pay into the common system for the common good --- paying and subsidizing  those who may need help now, knowing that if/when it's my turn to utilize needed medical service,  I won't have to worry about losing my house/declaring bankruptcy just so I can have a bed in the hospital.

I live in Canada and currently have been suffering a severe chest pain for months, because the doctors have been refusing to refer me to further testing except for basic blood tests since they believe my conditions aren't serious enough. 

That's how the system works in Canada. Because the healthcare is "free", there are severe shortages of medical professionals and facilities. And because there are severe shortages, your doctors will refuse you from any comprehensive testing or treatment like chest ultrascan unless you're about to die.

For such reason, under or misdiagnosis are common among Canadian doctors. And the goverment solves this issue by making doctors almost legally immune from any medical negligence charges or  lawsuits.

That's why many people outside Canada and even Canadians themselves are never largely aware of the horror situation we're in. All the clinical deaths from medical negligence due to the lack of medical fundings, staffs, and facilifies are recorded as unforseen deaths rather than medical  accidents.

To summarize, doctors will treat you only when you come to ER in critical conditions, or when you wait for symptoms get worse to the point of being life-threatening.

People who are arguing here that they'll rather wait for months or years to be diagnosed rather than being on thousand dollars of debt really have no idea what they're talking about. When you're dying or suffering from pain so much, money really is the last priority. 

Many Canadians seem to think their universal healthcare is the best in the world just because it's better than the American private healthcare. But the US isn't the only country in the world. Many Asian and European developed countries, like Japan, have far better systems of mixed social and private healthcares compared to the US and Canada. Why can't both the US and Canada  have the adequate mixes of social  and private healthcares as well?

text:
WHATS IN IT FOR THE GOVERNMENTs 

Europe vs America

European system costs 11% of GDP against 19% in the USA

EU HEALTH CARE IS ALL INCLUDED THE USA NOT

USA life expectancy is 42th in the world on the world ratings.

WHATS IN IT FOR THE EU PATIENT?

If you pay taxes, as an individual, and contribute to the central government “POT” (the community POT of money).
You are covered for all treatment, hospital costs, surgery, drugs, implants, chemo, post op therapies, cost of prescribed drugs, specialist and normal GP doctors bills, specialist bills etc etc.
(There are some small exceptions.)

You dont have to look at the small print of coverage.
Its all included, pocket your credit card.. you dont need it.
You dont have to pay in advance
NO Paperwork
The system is rated as better than the American system as nobody is excluded and the system covers 99% of all patient needs.

THE EUROPEAN SYSTEM:

HOW IT WORKS:
All european health systems are government run and are simple systems .
It is a central, run by the govenment covering all healthcare needs for the population and is non profit , universal, and also internationally recognised between all EU countries.
The EU underlying system is through fiscal residency of a person, (ie you pay into the employment tax system of the country you live and into health system, if you are registered or unemployed you are covered)
Simply put, each person pays into the THE GOVENMENT POT as a % of individual pay packet contributions where you are employed
Importantly even if you are unemployed, but registered, you control illegal immigration. Illegals get no benefits, unless humanitarian emergency.
Importantly you are still covered. you as a patient get equal rights and coverage across Europe.

The EU system is state run, and covers everyone, from birth to death and costs half of the USA system.
Therefore in the USA a % of the health cost is based on profit to be paid out in dividends.

Additionallly not all people in the USA are covered, (approximately 15%)
Its a Bum deal for Americans. It costs 8-9% of GDP more than Europe

HOW IT IS MANAGED:
In America USA, the health system is profit run for the most part and privately handled. Patient care is secondary to profit for 50% of coverage.
The European state (country) is the employer of all health staff, doctors, nurses, etc.

Central government then covers the hospitals costs through a system which keeps the hospitals covered for the expenses of each patient.
If we compare the EU to the USA structure in terms of cost to GDP, Europe average 11% of GDP to the USA 20% of GDP, (2019) with the EU at 99% coverage and with improved life expectancy rate, compared to the USA.
Central Government in each country collects the money through the taxes you pay as a citizen, and redistributes it (generally) to regions ,hospitals, and doctors etc which then distributes the funds to the health system used by the patient.
The Government also allows the private sector to operate under the public system, to build hospitals, run them privately.
Each country allows this in different rules, like Germany/UK/France etc. They are then licence them to operate by the government.
But importantly, the patient who pays to the state contributions through his salary, has equal rights to access all hospitals.
10-15 % of the EU health system is private

If you wish to pay an additional supplement to the state system, you are allowed to do this into to the private scheme on top of your mandatory state payments so you get private health care coverage.

But you cannot opt out of the central system or add to it.
Most of people dont have the means or desire to pay private insurance policies. So the state system is better for them.

WHATS THE BENEFIT
Basically the EU system covers 99% of people and costs the half of the USA system.
In addition to this, with the EU system, if you pay contributions to the health system in the UK or France, you can have full medical care in other countries , Germany, Italy, Hungary or any other of the 27 states etc.

If you lose your job, you will still be covered. you just pay less to the system as unemployment reimbusement to you is less, so you pay less, but the more you earn at work, the more you contribute to the central system. Its based on % of earnings.

These European systems are based on the old and original UK system, The National Health Service, abbreviated to NHS, was launched by the then Minister of Health in Attlee's post-war government, Aneurin Bevan, at the Park Hospital in Manchester. Aneurin Bevan, Minister of Health, on the first day of the National Health Service, 5 July 1948 at Park Hospital, Davyhulme, near Manchester.

THE REAL ISSUE
1. EU Health is non political, non profit, more efficient and cheaper that the USA system,

2. BETTER in many ways, as the patient is first, and health has no “profit COST”.

3. It reduces the cost to the Government. (11% against the USA 20% per year GDP)

4. Its not fragmented. Its coverage is national, and for all.

5. If you are REGISTERED as a TAX payer, you have no problems.

6. If you are clandestine, then you get some emergency humanitarian services only.

Thus the authorities know who you are and send you home after.

text: I have been researching various health care systems for some time. The Canadian system seems to be better than the US system, but it still seems to have big problems. I come from Germany and here the costs of the medication are covered by the insurance. This is important because the medication often costs more than exams, tests or hospital stays. In addition, we do not have a "free health system". There are statutory health insurances and private health insurances. The statutory health insurance companies are quasi companies, but they have to admit everyone, even in the case of previous illnesses etc. There is an obligation here in Germany to take out insurance. The employer pays 50% of the costs. If you are unemployed, social insurance covers the costs. This means everyone is insured, even if they are poor. How much you pay depends on how much you earn. There are private health insurance companies for people with higher incomes. Some of them cost more, but offer advantages such as single rooms in the hospital, shorter waiting times and experimental treatments. However, this does not mean that treatment is bad for those insured under the law. I am insured by the statutory health insurance myself. When I had surgery I got the best care. The whole story cost over 40,000 euros - and I didn't have to pay anything. After I am healthy again, I gratefully pay my contribution so that other people are helped so well.

text:
Anytime Americans discuss the Canadain health care system it's difficult not to immediately consider the negative claims are intentional so Americans don't get too excited. I have phone access to a doctor 24/7 with the potential for more if deemed necessary. I dropped dead in a grocery store of a cardiac arrest, fortunately, a police officer revived me. That day I had three cardiac arrests and was revived, two stents were installed, spent a week in ICU in a coma on life support before waking. One week after the day, I was discharged and walked out of the hospital. I have never seen an invoice for anything.  
Something that so often fails to be considered is the number of Americans that hold off seeking medical care and potentially make their ailments worse. It has been suggested the numbers of Americans who might be experiencing symptoms of the Coronavirus is not accurate due to Americans avoiding seeking care due to the cost. I watched a program that followed a husband and wife, they had both been diagnosed with some form of cancer. Not only were they unable to work but they had to sell their home and move into their daughter's guest room. Canadians don't have to sell their home or even consider it. And who wouldn't rather be in their own bed recovering.?
Anyone who might think it reasonable to argue healthcare can't be a possibility for all needs to educate themselves of the facts. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zvLay2dzYCM&t=64s  All Americans, including the rich, would benefit greatly if top tax rates went back to Eisenhower-era levels, a time when the top federal income tax rate was 91 per cent, and also a time when there was actually a middle class to speak of. The marginal tax rate that benefits all Americans, citizens, including the highest 1 per cent of earners is somewhere between 85 and 90 per cent. Unfortunately, decades of the gospel that tax cuts for the highest income earners are good has caused considerable harm to the nation and those living in it. Why does no one ever question the military budget.? There is always a constant, "where will the money come from," for healthcare but never the things that could be. 
The trend began with President Kennedy, though his cuts were hardly radical. He did lower rates when the American economy was no longer paying for World War II. Kennedy cut rates to around 70 per cent, a level we can hardly imagine raising them to today. Unfortunately, the huge drops -- from 70 per cent to 50 per cent to less than 30 per cent -- came with the Reagan presidency. 
A 90 per cent top marginal tax rate doesn’t mean that if you make $450,000, you are going to pay $405,000 in federal income taxes. Americans have well-documented trouble understanding the notion of marginal tax rates. The marginal tax rate is the amount you pay on your income above a certain amount. Right now, you pay the top marginal tax rate on every dollar you earn over $406,750. So if you make $450,000, you only pay the top rate on your final $43,250 in income. A top marginal tax rate in the range of 90 per cent would decrease both income and wealth inequality, bring in more money for the government and increase everyone’s well-being -- even those subject to the new, much higher income tax rate. 
The US is the largest wealthiest nation in the developed world and the only one that doesn't offer healthcare, that should motivate questions. 
FACT: The tax rate for a married couple with one income and two kids in the U.S. is 12 times as high as the tax rate on the same family in Canada. Canadians have universal healthcare. When Canadians get sick they receive medical care and are NEVER required to pay a bill, they don't even see an invoice. New data from the OECD shows the old belief Canadians are higher-taxed than Americans is no longer true. The employee net average tax rate for a single person in Canada with no children was 22.8 per cent in 2017, the 11th lowest among 35 OECD countries. The U.S. clocked in at 26.1 per cent. The OECD's estimates take into account federal and provincial or state taxes, as well as social security contributions and money in the form of family benefits.
Canadians get a lot more from their government in terms of social services. That’s part of what makes Canada one of the Top 10 happiest countries on earth, ranking seven spots higher than America.
American marginal tax brackets aren’t too different from Canadians’, except one has universal health care and the other doesn't. Currently, Americans pay $3.4 trillion a year for medical care and, unfortunately,  the U.S. life expectancy of 78.8 years ranks 27th. It has the fourth-highest infant mortality rate in the OECD, the sixth highest maternal mortality rate and the ninth highest likelihood of dying at a younger age from a host of ailments, including cardiovascular disease and cancer.
Per capita, health-care spending in the U.S. is more than $9,000. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HwjRkka7pAU
By contrast, per capita health-care spending in Canada is half that, or $4,500. Yet life expectancy in Canada is 81.7, and the country ranks 13th, significantly ahead of the U.S.
Of course, there are the critics of Canada’s single-payer health-care system who call it a “cautionary tale, ” a “failing" one. 
They claim it’s universal and affordable, sure, but the quality isn’t high. And the waits for procedures can be unnecessarily long: BUT, as Forbes has pointed out, “In 2013, the average wait time for an MRI was over two months, and Canadians needing a CT scan waited almost a month. ” But no Canadian waits for anything if it's a medical emergency and maybe what needs to be a greater consideration is waiting a month for a CT scan really a big deal if it's all at no cost. If there are never concerns about pre-existing conditions or penny-pinching for monthly payments for many having to wait doesn't strike as quite the problem those who profit on the current system try to make it. 
Overall, Canadians enjoy the kind of perks Americans only get if they work for the most generous, prestigious corporations. Those include free health care without deductibles as well as up to 18 months of subsidized parental leave when they have children. text:
This article is factually incorrect (hint, don't ask an American author to represent another country). 
In fact, 100% private medical service is completely legal in Canada. What you can't do is mix them. So if a doctor chooses to set up a wholly private practice, then they cannot submit claims to the provincial health care system ever. If you think you can drum up enough customers, you can open private clinics. But to put it in perspective, one such clinic in Calgary charges its customers around $30,000 a year subscription.. although they get 24/7 on demand service, full in-house test equipment like MRI, so no waiting, and private high end ward rooms if needed. Obviously their target market isn't the general public.
Not one cent of their operations come from the provincial health system and they can't split service and treat regular people then bill the gov.
Not surprisingly, there aren't many such places in Canada  because we don't have that many really rich people who could afford it.
Also, Canada keeps costs down by using front line triage. You can't just demand to see a specialist - you have to go to a GP first who will then refer you to a specialist if you need it. In the same way, you can't tell the doctor what tests to do. Resources are allocated by need, not by want.
Two weeks ago I pinched a nerve in my shoulder. I went to a walk in clinic without an appointment. less than ten minutes later I was with a doctor who did a quick exam and sent me off for x-rays and bloodwork. He also prescribed NSAID anti-inflammatories and some painkillers. I immediately went to the nearest lab, about five blocks away, got the x-rays done in ten minutes and the bloodwork took about another twenty. Again, all without any appointments. The doctor got the results back and called me to arrange an appointment to discuss the results which happened a few days later. He decided I needed an MRI and that I should see a neurosurgeon and renewed my painkillers and added one to help me sleep. The outpatient center called me a day after that to schedule the MRI, but I missed the call and am waiting for a callback. 
Total cost? $0 for the doctor's time and the labwork,.. $50 for the meds, which would have been free if my company health plan had started. If I need surgery - that also will be free. If I need a hospital bed...again free.
What people miss about the American system is that around 17% of Americans have no health coverage at all and rely on 'crashing' ERs to get treatment. They're all on 'waiting lists' because they can't pay and may never see a doctor. It's estimated that 50% of all Americans are under-covered, and so can only get basic or limited care. Even with coverage, the often high co-pays of the American system can cause people to put off seeing a doctor when they really should see one. This just doesn't happen in Canada, so it's a bit disingenuous to use  the 'waiting line argument.'
Also, there is no concept of 'in-system' doctors. All doctors (except the very rare private clinics I mentioned earlier) are in-system and you can go any one of them if they're taking new patients - which is determined by the doctor, not the system. There's also many clinics that take walk in patients. Because we have a unified system, your medical records are accessible by any registered doctor in the system, so it's easy for a clinic to check your history. The result is that Canadians actually have MORE choice than Americans where it counts.

text:
Reading the comments made me realize that many of my thoughts about this video are shared with others.


STATISTICS.  It would have been good to see more drilling down into those statistics.  It would be interesting to note if maternal and infant mortality is higher due to the remote nature of many communities.  The same would be true if factors such as remote areas of the USA, such as Alaska, were factored in.  At age 65, I do not know one person, friend, family, neighbour, co-worker who lost a mother or child during childbirth. 


WAIT TIMES.  When you book an appointment, normally you get in soon after unless something odd has happened (e.g. bad weather).  I don't expect to be able to call my doctor and get in to see him within 48 hours.  His schedule is already full.  But then I don't expect to do that for many things such as car repair either.  If I am feeling very ill then in most areas there are walk-in clinics where they look at you on a first come - first serve basis and you might wait hours to be seen but you will be seen.  Hospital emergency rooms are always an option too but if your illness is not life-threatening then you can wait a long time.  If you come into the emergency room with an actual emergency, you go to the top of the list and are seen.  Thus the non-emergency patient gets knocked down one more tier and waits longer.


RED TAPE.  I'm not sure what this even means - certainly not for the patients which was the implication.   I go to the doctor, I get examined, I get a prescription then I go to a pharmacy and have it filled.  I pay for the medication fully if I don't have a plan or only pay my premium and the pharmacy charges the rest to  my private drug plan.  At no point do I do anything other than use my debit or credit card to pay for my medication.  Doctors might have red tape but I suspect that is the case in all countries especially in those with private health insurance where every claim would have to be examined to assure it meets that particular insurance company's requirements.


PERSONAL COSTS.  I pay for a plan for drugs, dental and eye treatment.  It is not much considering what I could need it for.  Fortunately in my case I rarely need these services.  Now that I've turned 65 I am on a provincial plan for medication but will likely still pay into my private plan for the other benefits.  I am not sure of the USA but on the Canadian income tax form, if you did have to spend money out of your own pocket (for you and your partner) on any medical treatments including dental etc. or equipment (e.g. walker) then once it exceeds $2400 then those costs are tax deductible.


MENTAL HEALTH.  I have American friends who have said that they can envision situations where their life savings or their house could be lost due to medical expenses.  That is just inconceivable to Canadians.  Recently I've been thinking this might be the SINGLE greatest advantage of socialized medicine so have started to ask my American friends, "How often do you think about medical expenses?"  Some have said almost daily while others say certainly before big events, vacations, switching jobs etc.  Just being cautious that they are covered.   It is not that Canadians or Americans think any more or less about their personal medical conditions or that of our partners and children but in Canada, most simply never think about how to pay for something.    I now think the constant worry, even if in the background, about medical expenses is actually the most harmful aspect of a private insurance system.  Most Canadians never think of that let alone worry about it.

text:
An fairly honest presentation of Canada's healthcare, I'd say.  And yes, unless there is medical urgency, it can take awhile to see specialists or get elective surgery.  On the other hand, when it really counts the system steps up pretty well.  In 2010 I was diagnosed with a malignant but relatively slow moving colon cancer.  Twenty days later I had successful surgery to remove it.  This was followed by six months of chemo.  All covered 100% by Medicare.  I paid a couple of hundred dollars for anti-nausea drugs.  But that was it for personal expense.  If I'd had to pay for it myself, full costs would have been way over $100 000 and financially crippling for me.

As for "red tape",  I can't imagine what is meant.  The most I ever do is present my healthcare card, sometimes sign something -- sometimes not -- and that's it. I don't understand how that translates into "red tape"..?

A perfect system?  No.  Better than the US?  Oh, yeah.  Not even close...

text:
A few errors in this video.  One is that if a patient goes to emergency room thinking he or she has had a stroke after many blood problems, the waiting time is usually 9 to 13 hours because they claim there almost always no beds available.  Waiting time of the standard 2 to 4 hours is usually for small things like the flu or a broken foot or whatever.  Another is that many wealthy patients do indeed jump the que.  Another is that waiting for an MRI or to see a specialist is usually six months to a year after a patient has begged for a referral for several months.  Another is that several specialists under the two tier system do operate at both levels, although many patients are unaware because most doctors are too afraid to tell their patients that. 

If you don't pay money to deal for a long time through a separate company the doctor works from, which also appears in part to be tied into the public system,  few will ever qualify for a required operation, although the health system gets very angry when patients or doctors mention that.  In fact the system will be so angry they will call the GP to scold him for mentioning it, although most patients who have had surgery did indeed make the payment which is usually around $450 above the cost the government covers for spinal surgeries, laparoscopies, or knee surgeries.  (Probably many more types or surgeries as well but I personally know of the three).  It's the same with dental billing.  Most dentists charge the government rate but many can and do charge extra for the identical procedures and the patient himself pays the extra.  Tests such as MRI's, x'rays, CT scans or anything involving radiation, magnetic fields, etc. Are usually easy to get without extra cost but when the tests show serious problems, patients are often not treated until the conditions' have advanced beyond repair and they are crippled for life, especially in patients over 65.

This is what happened to me as a Canadian suffering from osteoarthritis.

At 18 I started to suffer from severe left hip pain.

My family doctor took a year to decide it was something he couldn't treat. A single CAT showed osteoarthritis. I had minor surgery to soften the tissues around the joint 3 months later.

Cut to 30 years later and the pain was becoming intolerable after many years of living a relatively normal life. I was limping as well.

Working at my job was very hard.

I was feeling a lot of anger and disenchantment from both my disease and the way the system was treating me.

In a few days I was set up with an orthopedic specialist and after reviewing my x-rays was shocked that I had handled such pain and bone growth for so long. 

380 days later(the then Liberal government of Ontario stated the average wait times was only 60 days, but I digress) I had my left hip resurfaced and it changed my life. text: Canadians do not have "socialized medicine".  We do have socialized health insurance. The video above calls it "a publicly funded model with private delivery". That's accurate - medical insurance is a public, non-profit monopoly that puts everyone in the same insurance pool. Everyone is covered and there is no private profit, so there is no need to discriminate against anyone by denying coverage to the sickest people or refusing claims. This saves us a colossal amount of money in comparison with the US health sector, which is burdened with a huge private bureaucracy whose only purpose is to deny coverage wherever possible in order to maximize profit and executive compensation.

text:
The "wait times" business has far less to do with the system we have and far more to do with being the worlds second largest geographical expanse and a tiny and spread out population. This means that guaranteeing European-level access equitably would be astronomically expensive regardless of whether it's public, private, or whatever. As such, there is a distribution hierarchy based on perceived level of urgency... meaning if you need to see a specialist because you might be terminally ill, you get in fast even if you need to be flown to another city. If you're waiting to see a specialist because you want to know how to treat an annoying rash on your skin or something, you might need to wait a bit.

When your civic mandate requires providing equitable access to care regardless of where the citizen lives, and have many communities so remote as to be without year-round road access (and all variants in between), you have to make these kinds of choices. That's not politics; it's math.

text:
One thing that the video kind of fudged is that while wait times are higher in Canada, if you have an URGENT need of a scan, surgery, appointment, etc., you get in more quickly. My mother had a bad kind of skin cancer (Merkel cell carcinoma) that was caught (fortunately) really early, and she had it taken out within a couple of weeks, and is doing fine now, 18 months later. So if you're getting a knee replacement or something, yes, you will likely wait between 9 months and a year, because the condition isn't threatening your life. If you want to get a mole removed because you are concerned it will turn cancerous later in life (but it shows no signs of being so now), that's a year wait, but if your primary care physician has noticed that it's abnormal NOW (maybe it's raised and has irregular edges), the wait time drops substantially (3-4 months max, and if it's REALLY abnormal, we're talking a week or two).

But as was alluded to, the wait times are not a consequence of the single-payer nature of the system, but of the limited funds us Canadians are prepared to spend on health care. If we spent as much as Americans do per capita, our wait times (for all procedures) would be equivalent to the US, but in Canada, the same, high level of care, would be available to all citizens (and with much better outcomes). So it's a question of what people are prepared to pay for.

One more point: the pharmaceutical issue is a tough one, but most provinces have a program (usually called Pharmacare) that covers drug expenses under certain circumstances. Where I live, the government sets a deductible (which varies depending on your income) beyond which drugs are free (any drugs paid for by private insurance don't count towards the deductible though). So if you're making 40,000 dollars a year, you might have to spend 1,500 dollars on drugs (out of pocket) and then they'd be free. But if you're making 10,000 dollars a year, your deductible would be 200-300 dollars. Thus, even people in the middle class, when beset with high prescription drug costs (say their kid has a rare disease or something), aren't going to break the bank paying for those drugs. If they don't have private insurance (and most employed people DO), they will have to pay a pretty high cost (say the drugs run maybe, 10,000 dollars a year or something, *they might pay half of that,* if their combined income is in the $70-80,000 range), which is substantial, but it's still far less than an uninsured family in the US would pay in the same situation. Plus...the drug prices are (often substantially) lower here. There's a reason so many Americans make trips to Canada to buy drugs...since the government negotiates as a unit with the drug companies, and there is no other entity to compete with them, they have a tremendous amount of power. If a company wants to sell its product here, it has to deal with the government, and therefore, even if you do end up paying out-of-pocket, it's so much less here than in America.

Basically, it's complicated. We know that the system isn't perfect, but enough people are happy with it the way it is (i.e. they're unwilling to pay more taxes to get a better system) that it's not going to change in any drastic way anytime soon. And doctors seem to be happy with it too...while we did experience a bit of a brain drain of medical staff in the '90s, now we find that some years more doctors are COMING to Canada to practise from the US, than are leaving. They make a decent living, plus most don't have horrendous medical school expenses...where I live, tuition will run you 22,000 USD TOTAL for a 4-year degree (at many American schools, a single year costs more than twice as much as that).

Let me add perspective from a Canadian. 1st of all our system is not called Medicare or anything like that. It has different names depending on the Province. Usually Pharmacare. It is a very good system because if you need hospitalisation you are covered equally, rich or poor. It’s actually great. But, outside of needing hospitalisation, our version of Medicare / Medicaid) called Pharmacare (or OHIP IN Ontario, and RAMQ in Quebec), there are huge disparities in cover from Province to Province. As brought out in this video, in Canada you need private insurance cover as well. If you can’t afford it, ie you are poor, you will not have the same access to drugs, optical, mental, dental services as the rich. 
Why do we pay less? One reason is that we have huge wait times. If you go to the hospital and it’s not an emergency, it is not unusual to wait 6 to 8 hours soo see a Dr. You may be in the excruciating pain....too bad. Hip or knee replacement...6 to 9 months...MRI...2 to 3 months (non emergency- say you tear all the ligaments in your knee). Another reason is that we don’t get the latest, most up to date drugs and treatment options available in the US. Thirdly, drugs in the US are generally much more expensive than in Canada (same drug). text: Lmfao being Canadian I can tell you the healthcare system is overrated compared to being billed as better than the US. Canada has the same issues with regards to rising out of pocket prescription drug and private insurance costs for mental, dental and vision care. The universal system does not cover prescription drugs for anyone under 65 outside immediate hospital/emergency room care. There are some piece-meal programs for the very poor but the rest pay a lot out of pocket or with higher Private insurance premiums, deductibles and co-payments. Add to that healthcare expenditures tend to be the largest line item in provincial budgets (like US state budgets). They are largely funded through higher provincial sales taxes (compared to US states) and Health transfer payments from the Federal Government. Those Federal Health Transfer Payments to provinces are largely financed by Canada's 5% Federal GST - a nationwide sales tax in addition to provincial ones and something the US has never had. Healthy and young Canadians pay an arm and leg through higher sales taxes on their everyday purchases for their universal health coverage. It is really only the sick and elderly Canadians who get the most service (Canadians over 65 get free drug coverage) that are getting the best deal because in the US they would likely be paying ridiculous health insurance premiums over pre-existing conditions that would greatly exceed any taxes they have paid in Canada. Notice how the countries with two-tier healthcare like Australia, Sweden, UK and the Netherlands perform much better than Canada in all the quality indicators - they maintain both a public and private healthcare system that helps move the wealthy off of billing the taxpayers for their healthcare in exchange for shorter wait times. Without this option all Canadians suffer with ridiculous wait times while the wealthy Canadians still spend their money on quality private healthcare just in the US (so money is leaving Canada).

One of the biggest problems with the US system is that there are tons of procedures you can get done that have questionable effectiveness. And most people are unable to evaluate the efficacy of a procedure. This unfortunately also turns many doctors into glorified salespeople, selling you procedures you don't need. 

Sure, you can get in to get your knee replaced next week. This is more true than ever with Trump's cuts to the FDA.

By contrast, Canada is much more conservative. This kind of experimentation should only occur in clinical trials with full consent of the patient. And devices/techniques/procedures which don't make the cut will never become standard practice.

text:
Canadian here, gonna admit it:  Canada's health care system is a solid C.  ...And yes, I believe the US system is a D.  And the video did a really great job at pointing out both of these facts.... But I want to say that no one in the US should be looking up to Canada's health care system, and Canadians should not be so uppity about themselves.  Unfortunately I grew up so proud of it too...because Canadians are constantly bombarded about how great we are compared to the U.S. ....but here's the thing: you would never applaud a child for getting a C on their test.  
And here's what none of the graphs in this video even considered:  There is a world outside of North America and Europe.  

I have been living in Asia for the past 13 years, and yes, this is me about to tell people from both of these countries that health care in Asia is better.  
China is not as communist as you think.  I give their system a shocking B, just because I could afford to buy really good health care in a two-tier system (and in reality, I paid very little, it would only be Chinese lower-middle-class standards that it would be considered an expense).... if you can't afford that good health care though, I'm sure a poor Chinese citizen would give it a much lower grade.  
Taiwan, though sometimes seeming to be a still-developping country, has very good health care.  It is affordable, available on off hours, many hospitals and are well staffed, and the medication is included in your trip to the doctor.  Though hospitals tend to be quite crowded and a little bit slower than my hopes.  B+
Japan was disappointing.  Their health care system is too complicated (and the non-optional national health care system is not cheap!) and not widely available in off hours.  I actually give it a B- 
South Korea ....  A.  This is the country that needs more attention world wide for their health care success.  It is affordable, though there are (very minor) out of pocket expenses.  There are no shortage of hospitals all over the country and many many specialty clinics - all providing excellent care with a speedy intake and readily available surgery options.  Whatever South Korea is doing right needs to be looked into by other countries.

1. Covered procedures are determined by the provinces, at least to some extent.

text:
how does it work? Badly.  That is how.   Sure it is nice when you break a leg, but, just wait until you have been mis diagnosed, or you have to wait more than a year for a knee surgery and grow 4 " without being able to move your leg.   Worst thing? No other choices.  I have had a problem on the sole of my foot for 14 years.  It takes a year to get into the system whereby i see a specialist, but i have no choice over whom i see, and little over when.   Then when i do get treatment and start to get better, they stop treatment, and instead of curing me, they get to milk the system by dragging it out.  Takes me another 9 months to get back in.  Rinse and repeat a dozen times, with me traveling across the country and taking a week off work each time.

You know what else sucks?  Since it is "free" all sorts of idiots wreck themselves skiing or mountainbiking, thus clogging up the system for people who need to do physical work to make money.
Furthermore, it should be noted that the cost of healthcare has gone up year after year, with no exception.   It is a gross portion of our taxes.   Actually getting a doctor is nearly impossible.
Last time i hurt myself ( actually a problem caused by a previous surgery that went badly due to having to wait so long for the surgery in the first place) I spent 6.5 hours waiting in ER (on one foot, because it was standing room only) behind a bunch of yuppies who hurt themselves in ski accidents. 

Also, be prepared to have all decisions be made by centralized governments, with little regard to local people.  My small town has more than enough money to have an excellent hospital, yet despite having paid for one, we get a skeleton staff and neglected because the system has centralized everything into a hospital more than an hour away.  Even actual things donated to the local hospital by charities have been appropriated and sent out of region. 

The last government decided to pander to the larger voting block, and despite our region paying an outsized amount of tax, the services are provided elsewhere.


And another thing, again you have no choice of where you are sent.   My father needed very specialized care, and there is a large hospital that is an easy 4 hour drive away, but not in province.   He got sent to a different hospital 14 hours away, and then promptly ignored in a corner room for weeks.  Despite the crap care (it got better as he befriended the staff ofc) he had 0 choice in perhaps going somewhere else.


America and canada both have objectively bad systems.  We need to look at people other than each other for solutions.   Cuba has some great ideas related to how doctors are trained (in canada we have a sort of guild that ensures short supply- and many students wasting a lot of money for nothing).  Mixed public/private nations offer the universality of care without the stifling overcontrol- often subbing things out to the private sector when cheaper.  Mexico offers excellent elective treatments for things, often done to canadian medical tourists by american doctors. ( a commentary on the issues with both our systems)


America needs to get the lawyers, insurers, accountants, lawyers, and managers, and lawyers out of its system, they add no net benefit while being the biggest portion of the cost. 

Canada needs to allow more doctors and nurses more freedom, as well as streamline our education process.  We also need to allow patient choice in where and how they get care, as well as removing all the managers in our system.  Most hospitals in canada have nicer and newer offices than they do outpatient areas.


The growth of all sorts of industries around the edge of the healthcare system in canada should tell people something : people are looking for solutions they can't get in a canadian hospital. 

Things like chiropractors, various therapy people, physiotherapists, and health/wellness guru's peddling herbs are often peoples first answer to go see rather than a hospital or doctor.   A nice example of the free market providing people what they need at a much better price, shame actual medicine can't be practiced in such a manner. 

My doctor (who i think is pretty good) can order liquid nitrogen through the health system at pricing that is something along the lines of hundreds of dollars per litre, while a higher grade is available industrially for freezing bull/stallion sperm and is priced along the lines of dozens of dollars per 50L.  The difference?  government cost Vs. Market cost.

Great Britain would do well to move over to the Canadian form of health care. The British NHS is a behemoth that doesn't work as well as the Canadian system.  The charts showing international comparisons in this video show that in the UK outcomes are in most categories poorer than in Canada. text:
I’d like to know - where are all of the “long lines” and frightful “red tape” I’m apparently dealing with to access Canadian healthcare? In my 59 years, I’ve never experienced a wait for a doctor’s visit or a medical procedure; even during my visits to hospital emergency rooms I’ve been seen within 2 hours at the most.

Seven years ago at the age of 52, I was diagnosed with a severe, chronic autoimmune disease. I was in the middle of switching to a new family doctor (yes, we get to choose our own physicians, no one tells us what clinic or Dr’s office we have to use) who did an excellent job of coordinating a plethora of tests, etc (all at no cost to me) with my old one. In just a couple of weeks I was being seen by specialists at two world-class hospitals. Although I was forced to medically retire from work, my health has stabilized due to ongoing, excellent physician & specialist care. My doctor’s visits and medical tests, including regular bloodwork & ultrasounds, are all provided to me at no cost. My prescriptions are so heavily subsidized that even on a fixed income, I’m assured that my financial situation will remain stable, no matter when my remission ends.

Get on board, America. No one deserves to go bankrupt, become homeless, or go without food simply because they have the misfortune to become ill or be the victim of an accident. You deserve to pay less for your healthcare while ensuring that your neighbours also have coverage. You’ll all be better off and happier.

Love from British Columbia, Canada!

text: I am a born and raised Canadian, and I've been fortunate enough to live and work in the US, South Korea as well as Australia where I am a permanent resident, and have experienced the medical system in these countries and more. I fractured my finger when travelling overseas (surfing, don't feel bad for me lol), came back fo Canada and because my case wasn't urgent as the finger had already started healing, I had to wait 6 weeks for the surgery. I was 100% fine with this, because it's based on priority. There were other people in more need of care, and there were people who probably had to wait longer than me because their case wasn't so serious. I got my free surgery, all follow ups and physiotherapy free. Then I had to head back to the US where my finger had an infection and I had to visit the hospital in Denver. A single hospital visit came up to $4000. Luckily, I have insurance so they paid, but the hospital messed up all the payments and it took a year and a boatload of paperwork to correct. In the end, they wanted us to pay an additional $800 which was incorrect. I was curious to see the process through and not give into the bullying, so I fought it with my insurance company and won. I can't imagine Americans having to go through this for a single visit to emergency, and for a nurse to look at you, give you an injection and send you home. I am ever, ever so grateful just for the mere fact I don't have to deal with any paperwork when I go to any doctor in the Canada. An important missing point is usage rates.  It is a smaller percentage of that population that require medical assistance. The Canadian system works only because it was brought in so early and prices have been controlled since.

text:
Did you see how Australia came in second on the charts at every turn? Here's how Australia, a country comparable to the USA in physical size and values make our system (also called Medicare) work such that it delivers best outcomes;


The department of human services Medicare office decide what they are willing to pay for a particular diagnostic, procedure, surgery, etc. This creates the Medicare schedule at a federal level. States provide the hospitals and bill Medicare for all of their billable items at the scheduled rate. This ensures if you need emergency care or surgery you can get it. However on a day to day basis this is not the primary way Medicare is accessed. Private doctors, specialists and diagnostic practitioners bill Medicare directly at the scheduled rate for the patients they see, this process is called Bulk Billing, or if they want to charge more than the Medicare schedule they bill the patient the difference. So, say you see a GP. Medicare currently has their billing rate at $180/15 minutes, if the practice is happy with that rate the patient has no out of pocket fee for seeing the doctor, at all. These doctors advertise themselves as bulk bill doctors and are where most Australians want to go. If a given GP isn't happy with the schedule, say they think they should be paid $220/15 minute appointment, the patient pays just $40 out of pocket. 


Same for blood tests, x-rays, opticians, mental health services, specialists, etc. Dental is covered for children 17 and under, and medications can be placed on the pharmaceutical benefits scheme (PBS) schedule where the medication is heavily subsidised by the federal government to an affordable amount. For example a cancer drug that might cost $4000 out of pocket to the patient in the USA would cost $50 to the patient in Australia with the government picking up the tab for the remaining $3950. People on social security have further benefits making their out of pocket contribution in the same scenario never more than $5. 


Don't like the public system in Australia? That's ok, we don't discriminate against private care, in fact we actively subsidise it because patients in private care cost the government less freeing up money to improve public services care. Australians purchase private health insurance themselves the same they would any other financial product. We do not purchase through an employer, employers actively have to pay more tax if they provide health insurance to employees. It's something the consumer decides to do themselves at their own expense.   


Weirdly in addition to private health insurance covering private hospitals, optical, allied health, etc, you can also present to a PUBLIC hospital with private health insurance and pay privately. Because you're paying more the public hospital lets you skip the queue. This gives an incentive for people who can purchase private health insurance to do so, further subsidising the overall system. 


Wait times for GPs are not high here, there are LOTS of doctors. In fact within walking distance of my house there are no less than 7 GP practices each with multiple doctors inside them. I can get a GP appointment within the hour any day of the week. But I don't even have to leave my house if I don't want to, home visit GPs are plentiful and can be with you within 2 hours of your call. No out of pocket expense involved, completely bulk billed. Need to see a specialist but they're in another state or you live remote/rural? No problem, telehealth services are bulk billed giving you access to the best care no matter where you are. Heck, now days your surgeon doesn't even have to be in the same hospital as you, they can be in their rooms and remote control robotic arms. Bulk billed of course.      

Our system isn't perfect, most Australians would like dental included and more funding for mental health services with high demand to increase capacity. But overall as the figures here show, our system does very well. Given how comparable our countries are the US should look hard at our system and how we achieve such fantastic outcomes while spending significantly less than the USA or Canada.

text: There is a key statement that is made. It keeps admin costs low. Unfortunately in the US, the number of administrators has exploded while the number of physicians has either flatlined or declined. Hospitals in the US have become research centers, because then they get federal grant money and bragging rights to say they are the best. There is also another problem, the US does not have an equally informed and educated populace as a lot of these other countries. That information or knowledge problem means that there are fewer people there to check the power of anybody in power, be it a pharma lobby or a local mayor. In the US, if you have a medical emergency, and you go to the hospital, they are both legally and hippocratically required to treat you. Basically, supply and demand rule in all industries. If, the US makes a switch to government provided healthcare, then there are going to be fewer hospitals operating, and they will almost certainly all go to the wealthy areas where outcomes are higher and better doctors and staff are. Now, with all of that said, a true, non biased investigation into each hospital would be able to better shed light on the reasons for the general public.

My sincere condolences to this young lady's family🙏🌹❤️
 I'm going through something similarly, I'm 46 and have been healthy as an ox for my entire life up until recently. For that reason, the only time I was involved with doctors was prelabour and during my kids births. There was no need to go to the doctors.
Then 'something' happened  and swiftly and suddenly I was almost paralyzed by joint pain. The ER gave me prednisone right away, and then after I was done a short course of it I could feel it was coming back again so I went to the GP closest to me. He just continued on it and didn't even question why it was happening, saying because of my age it has to be RA. RA is a genetic disease and nothing like that runs in my family.I said by their own definition it couldn't be RA, it HAS to be a symptom of something else. I wasn't heard.
Now, 2 years later I have mass rashes all over my legs from the medication given by the RA specialist. And there are other symptoms I have that clearly are pointing to the fact I most probably have Lyme disease. I keep trying to tell my GP this and he won't even give me a Lyme test to ease my fears. 
It feels like they're masking symptoms but not finding the actual point of origin and I really believe I have Lyme disease. Trying to get them to listen is like pulling teeth😔 so frustrating

text: The public healthcare system is definitely not working. I've been waiting for my medical appointment for more than 6 month to just get an appointment to see the specialist. I have experienced the medical services in a couple of countries, the waiting time of Canadian healthcare is definitely the most ridiculous one. In most provinces the residents are not allowed to seek for private healthcare service even if they are under a critical situation. As a result, if you live on the west coast, the choice you have is either cross the southern boarder or fly 5 hours to Montreal. The patient have no control over the referral process. They are often send to the worst ones with lowest ratings you can find online (1 out of 5 stars), since the waiting time of these kind of doctor are usually shorter. In the United States, everybody can directly go to talk to a specialist without a referral from family doctor, patients not the doctors can decide which specialist you are going to see. One can easily use the ratings on the Internet to find the best healthcare providers available. Low-rated health care providers will not be able to sustain their clinic and is going to be swept out of the health care market, since they will get no customers. The government is trying to bring in millions of new immigrants will the supply of new doctors, nurses and medical equipments are not keeping up with it. On the other hand, a large number of the doctors and nurses are moving to work in the United States, where they can get double or triple of their current salary in Canada. Some of the important medical equipments in the hospitals are more than 50 years old with outdated technology. A comprehensive reformation is inevitable. As a complete change of the current Canadian medical system would be hard, it would be good if Canadian citizens are allow to purchase private health insurance in the United States and solve their medical issues there.

text: It's interesting to see them featuring the one patient going to a country with a dual medical system. The type of system some Canadians want. I've lived in Timișoara for almost 4 years and have seen the huge failings of this type of a system. The private hospital I had insurance with here could not even stitch my finger up or assess my fractured thumb on an emergency basis. Off to the  regular emergency hospital where the x-ray equipment is from the 60's and everything is old and dilapidated.  The doctors are skilled and knowledgeable but they are working with old equipment and outdated medical supplies.  The average person here cannot ever think of affording any private medical care so they are stuck with public. Some hospitals have 1 or 2 bathrooms for everyone in a wing, not a room. The rooms are so small there is no privacy, truly 3rd world. There's a lot of corruption within the public sector, money meant for improvements just disappear. Here is the thing about this:

Some people think that having a private, two-tier system is the solution.

I can tell you that it's not because the US system is mostly private, and they pay some of the highest prices in the world for both essential, and elective care.

My wife's COBRA used to be $560.34 USD/month and that doesn't include the co-pays, the deductibles, nor the annual out-of-pocket maximum.

You get what you pay for.

And Canada can DEFINITELY be better managing the resources of the overall healthcare system.

text:
Like they say: FOLLOW THE MONEY! I myself am waiting for 2 surgical procedures. One I can't get at all, and waiting time for the other is 18 months! One of my previous surgeons stated the obvious: that there are 2 levels of health care in this country, one for the rich and one for the poor. If you have the money, immediate surgery is available in tons of places outside Canada. One lady I know arranged a knee operation within one week - in Hong Kong for $20,000. On the other hand, what can provincial governments do? Doctors make absurd amounts of money with OHIP.  My run-of-the-mill GP at a public clinic in Toronto makes about $340K a year. Their nurses make around $150K. And many specialists bill OHIP for well over $1 million a year. 

If you look at the Ontario Budget for 2022, health care, as minimal as it is, still takes over 40% of every tax dollar. If elected surgeries were added in, it surely would go up to 80% - or some number like that. Taxpayers would be rioting in the streets. I guess it'll always be like Our Lord said, "The poor ye shall always have with you."

Problem #1: Too much bloat in the healthcare system. There are way too many six-figure salaried paper pushers that contribute no value to front-line care delivery. They focus on job preservation and care little about individual patients or their health problems.

Problem #2: Horrific inefficiency and waste in the healthcare system. This is across the board: primary care, emergency room care, hospital based care, and surgical care. There is no vision or co-ordination amongst different care providers and settings. Ontario Health, the latest beurocratic monstrosity in Ontario, has predictably ended up being more beaurocratic bloat with little deliverables.

The machinery of the public healthcare system, at least in Ontario, is focused on duping the public into believing they are getting good healthcare. Whether they actually get high quality, safe care is moot--what matters is tricking citizens into thinking they are getting good care. It's all about hollow virtue signalling. text:
Trust me as an American the Health Care in the USA is terrible. Depending on where you live there is no doctors or specialists.  Or it takes 9mo to get in to someone. 

Do not listen to Trump he has NO idea the struggle and broken system average Americans deal with he is the 1% who has access at any time and as POTUS its only gotten better.


Health care workers are burnt out as they have to argue with insurance to justify medical needs. Insurance should never run shot over a Liscensed educated professional.  Our life expectancy is POOR.

Americans don't take meds to try to save meds. Americans save meds even expired meds just in case. 

People have to choose between food and other essentials vs health care. 

Insurance companies hold americans by the hearts and it should be illegal. 

Also, regarding wait times, The average wait times for basic Primary care can range 30min-2hrs, specialists regardless in person or remotely you can be waiting 1-5hrs before being seen.
In the ED wait times vary on insurance and who is more important based on triage which isn't always solid to go off of. I have waited a full 26hrs in ED. The least I have waited was 10hrs.

When I had an appendicitis I got in to the Urgent Care ER combo within 1.5hrs. However, once it was discovered Doctors asked if I could have my BF pick me up, because the ambulance would take 6hrs. My BF picked me up took me to the main hospital and we walked straight into the OR with the doctors calling ahead of course. 

For scheduled surgery and procedure at the university / teaching hospital you may be scheduled to come at 6am and you are not called back for surgery for up to 12hrs.

Colonoscopies as of June 2025 they were booking out to August 2026. 

Think of that all of those Americans some whom may already show signs of cancer and having to wait over a year is lost time and lost life. 


My brother in law had a major heart surgery was hospitalized for 9mo. Upon DC he was out in February his Cardiology follow up was booked in December eventhough he was told he must follow up within 1 month. He and my sister called daily especially as he started having some symptoms. His meds were making him very sick he wanted his meds changed. The said they couldn't do anything until his appt. He proposed to my sister in the hospital they got married in August he died just a couple months later in October of a massive heart attack.. They were together for 21yrs and finally approaching their 22nd year they got married just for their fairy tale to end. If only he could have walked in and immediately got the health care he was told to and tried to get..

Coming from an American the US Healthcare system is grossly flawed.

text:
52% of Americans have employer sponsored healthcare insurance. About 39% have accessed the subsidized market place healthcare insurance. Only 9% of Americans don't have healthcare insurance coverage. It's because 5% of them don't know they have access to subsidized market place healthcare insurance. The rest are undocumented immigrants have extremely limited access to healthcare coverage and are ineligible for most federal programs. Federal law requires hospitals to provide certain emergency services regardless of a person's ability to pay or immigration status, and some states offer limited, state-funded programs. 

Canadian taxpayers fund healthcare through general taxes, with a typical family of four paying an estimated $19,060 in 2025, while single individuals pay around $5,703, though costs vary by income and province, often appearing invisible as it's not a dedicated health tax. These costs are rising faster than food and housing expenses and are part of the total $399 billion projected for Canadian health spending in 2025, equating to about $9,626 per person. 

 My city's emergency waiting time average increased from 2 hours to 9 hours in the last 5 years. My coworker had to fly to India to get an MRI done since it was a 12 month waiting time since his case was deemed as insignificant in Canada. But, his doctor in India shared that he did his MRI scan on time, if not would have resulted in amputating his foot. For $9,626 I could get premium healthcare in the US.

text:
Well, I'm Canadian. And, sure, our healthcare is 'free' ... if you can access it. Like a lot of Canadians, I don't have a family doctor. And when I've had a medical emergency,  I've had to wait for 6 hours in the hospital emergency room to be seen by a medical technician. And 'free' translates into 'tax-supported', which means most provincial governments are struggling to cover healthcare costs ... hence the lack of doctors & nurses & hospital beds & state-of-the-art technology.

I'm retired, and living on CPP/OAS. It's a constant struggle. I'm actually considering selling up and moving to Colombia. The healthcare system in that 3rd world country is rated superior to ours in Canada. Yes, I'll have to pay a small percentage of my income for government health insurance, but at least I'll have better medical care, and a more affordable cost of living. Canadians are living in a fantasy world if we think we are a shining example of how to do things.

text:
As a Canadian citizen with seven years of experience living in the United States, I’ve seen both sides of the border, and the truth is that Canada’s "universal" healthcare is a broken, disorganized mess. While people praise the "free" aspect, you get exactly what you pay for: garbage.

The competency gap is staggering. In Canada, I couldn't even find a specialized cervical chiropractor, and finding a podiatrist in Ontario has become nearly impossible. Even basic procedures are a disaster, my wife had medical debris left inside her after delivery, and I’ve dealt with nurses who can’t even find a vein for routine blood work.

The wait times are a death sentence. I know someone with fibroids who has been told a 1.5 year wait just for an initial urology consultation is "normal." People are literally dying of heart attacks in ER waiting rooms while the system remains chaotic and unaccountable.

The quality and speed in the States are lightyears ahead. Even places like Dubai put Canada to shame now. The Canadian system is staffed by incompetent, indifferent professionals, and the public is too entitled to realize how far we’ve fallen. If you value your health and your family’s safety, the U.S. model, where you are treated like a priority rather than a number is the only way to go.
