text:
Canada’s healthcare system is publicly funded, meaning that essential medical services are available to everyone without direct charges at the point of care. This system reflects a societal choice: health is considered a right, not a privilege. Canadians don’t face bankruptcy when someone in their family falls seriously ill. They are shielded from the catastrophic financial consequences that can devastate households under a purely private system.
Critics often point to long wait times in Canada as a flaw, especially for elective procedures or specialist appointments. While these delays can be frustrating, they reflect the challenge of managing resources in a system designed to prioritize access for all, rather than the ability to pay. The trade-off is that Canadians do not risk losing their life savings, homes, or financial security to receive life-saving treatments—a risk that many middle-class families in the United States face every day.
In the U.S., healthcare is largely privatized and tied to insurance coverage. Millions of families experience medical bankruptcy, crippling debt, or foreclosure after a serious illness or accident. The financial pressure can be so severe that people delay or forgo care entirely, worsening outcomes. In contrast, Canada’s model may involve some delays, but it guarantees that no one has to choose between treatment and financial survival.
In essence, Canada’s system prioritizes collective security and health equity over speed and convenience, whereas the U.S. system prioritizes immediate access for those who can afford it, often at the cost of long-term financial stability for everyone else. The debate isn’t just about wait times—it’s about the value placed on human life and social protection versus the commodification of health.

text:
The Barbarian Invasions (Les Invasions Barbares) is the best and widest ranging social commentary movie I have ever seen.  A 2003 French Canadian movie about the death of an unaccomplished radical socialist professor and his regrets from a life badly lived.  He had been a selfish cad who indulged himself at every opportunity, forgoing his duty to wife, family and others whenever convenient.  Better people, whom he had treated badly, step up to comfort and aid him in his time of need.  

A doctor will tell you that it is about socialized medicine, but it is not. It is set against a backdrop of socialized medicine but is about all the stupid ism’s that were embraced by the baby boomer generation. It is a story of redemption of the father son relationship as his extremely competent estranged son (this guy really has his act together) at the urging of his mother, the professor’s ex-wife. The son intervenes and overcomes every obstacle an entrenched uncaring state bureaucracy can present.  If they had dubbed it in English rather than having subtitles, it would have been a big hit rather than the obscure film that it is. I recommend it.

text: "CANADA HEALTH CARE" doesn't exist, it's NOT a "Federal system".   The Federal government has legislated MINIMUM standards, and gives the Provinces a MINIMUM paytmen for their Healthcare.    Each Province runs their OWN healthcare system.  Some Provinces, like Alberta, put NO Provincial money into their system, and wait-times are terrible....  Others, like Ontario, healthcare is the largest line-item in the Provincial budget, and the 'wait times' at Family doctors' offices are negligible.....tests are performed by private clinics and billed to the Province, no waiting....aside from a few minutes in the office, depending upon the time of day.  Priority of referrals to Specialists/specialist clinics is determined by the Family Doctor.....not by insurance companies or the 'government".

text:
“The cost of healthcare in Canada, funded via taxes, is not directly billed to individuals, but estimates help clarify what each Canadian taxpayer effectively pays for public health care:
- In 2024, the average Canadian taxpayer is expected to contribute about $5,629 toward public health care insurance annually.
- For a family of four (two adults, two children) with an average household income, the annual contribution is roughly $17,713.
- Couples without children typically pay around $16,528 per year.
- Single parents with one child face costs of about $5,345 annually.
These figures, calculated by the Fraser Institute, reflect the portion of general taxes ultimately allocated to public healthcare, which most Canadians DO NOT SEE DIRECTLY abut pay as part of their overall tax bill. Public healthcare funding increases regularly and has risen faster than household income over recent decades.”

text:
I dislocated and broke my ankle this past May, and it took 20 minutes for the ambulance to arrive. They took a life-threatening call before mine, so definitely a difference in urgency.

In the hospital, it took them 30 minutes to put me in my own room waiting for my ankle to be snapped in place. That took around 2 hours, but again, other people were being attended and had bigger issues than a broken ankle.

They snapped my ankle in place and put on the first cast to keep my leg in place until the surgery, which happened 3 days later (incident was on Monday, surgery on Thursday). After surgery, they put on another cast that stayed in place for 6 weeks. After the 6 weeks, they changed casts to a new, fiberglass one.

I am starting to walk again and recovery is as well as it could be at this point.

Out of all of these, the Fiberglass Cast costed me CAD$100 and I got a full refund from the insurance and the Ambulance was CAD$45.00, which I could have refunded as well but was too lazy to do so.

My surgeon was a specialist in my kind of injury, so that also helps.

There are definitely flaws in the Canadian system, for sure, but when you actually need it, you will get the help that is required. And it won't cost you a car or a house to do it. We pay taxes for this treatment, it's not free per se, but it is still a way better system.

I should point out my wife broke her elbow last year and had a similar experience, but she didn't need a surgery.

text:
In the UK I had an issue with my knee. On the national service the wait time to get it checked was 4-6 months for a referral, then an estimated 4-6 months to see a physio on receiving a referral. I have a small level of private healthcare with my employment, I called them up and they scheduled me to see a physio in two days. 

People shouldn’t act like nationalised healthcare is perfect (it is arguably people who get out more than what they put in who think that), you have a myriad of problems: 1) longer wait times. 2) lower quality. 3) low paid staff, and general staffing issues. It is definitely worth having that safety net, but let’s not pretend it doesn’t have huge issues.

text:
s someone who has lived in both countries I can say that they both have their pluses and minuses.

First keep in mind that the system in Canada is not a national system. Each province runs their own insurance system so some provinces do it better than others. 

Emergency care or life-saving care will always be given immediately.  Waiting time to see a regular doctor or a specialist very but are not outrageous, depending on where you live.

What you tend to wait for is for non-life-threatening specialty care. But the good news is that because initial care is not going to break your bank. People tend to go to the doctor when it’s easier and less expensive to treat something rather than pushing it off until it costs a lot more.

Basically, they triage people based on need not on ability to pay.  And nobody worries about not having enough money to afford their care or that it will bankrupt them.

text: I live in Canada. Taking medical decisions is difficult, whether it's for oneself or for a loved one. text: In the U.S., unpaid hospital (and other medical bills) are sent to a collection agency, where your credit can be dinged. If you can't pay, they can garnish your wages, freeze your bank account, put a lien on your house or other personal property. Sometimes, you can work out a payment plan, and/or they'll reduce the amount owed. Biden and Congress pressured the big three credit reporting agencies to not lower your credit rating for any bills under $500. As for workplace insurance, even the best companies do not pay your entire premium (we paid over 400./month for one person). We're generally always happy to pay for workplace insurance (at least it wasn't from United Health). You also have to pay deductibles and co-pays. And, at least in larger cities, we often have to wait as long as three months to get a doctor's appointment, even as an established patient. And I've had some hours-long wait times in some doctors' offices. It's true that they can't turn you away from an Emergency Room, and that they have to have to treat you, but you will get a bill for those services. In a for-profit hospital E.R. you can easily wait for hours to be seen; in a non-profit hospital it's generally not that long. We know your health care isn't perfect, Canada, and there are legitimate complaints, but please forgive us if we don't sympathize a whole lot. Or your low prices for many drugs. I have to order the one drug I take from Canada, because I can't get it here, and I was blown away by the difference in price! We have close friends who have lived in Canada for decades, and they don't really complain about their health care to us; they know better. ;)  Oh, sorry about that b.s. tariff thing. We feel on edge like that every day.

text: I will take free health and wait any day of the week. Last week my little one needed to go to emerge and in Canada you get checked in, triaged, registered then get sent to the waiting area. Normally to get to your waiting area it takes not to much time time for them to a triage and register you because the wait to see the dr is usually 6 hours MINIMUM for non life threatening illnesses (this is why most Canadians avoid the ER at all costs, pun intended) Well on this day it took over 2 hours just to be checked in, triaged and registered so you can imagine how many hours or days we spent in the ER waiting to see a dr. Oh I forgot to add the reason I took my little one in was because she couldn’t breathe and was turning blue and purple, I was about to call 911 when she took a breathe but I still took her in to make sure if it happened again we were in the right place and because I wanted to find out why it happened. It was an emergency in my eyes but when they were checking us in they took pulse ox and it was normal so now my little one became not important. Oh forgot to mention after give or take 27 hours we seen the dr (very young, fit and was a nutrition over medicine type of person) so he looked me in my eyes after just looking at my little one and said verbatim “everything looks good, lots of fluids, rest and a honey, ginger, turmeric drink a day would be great” um sir my child was blue and purple and couldn’t breathe for apparently no reason, you never did a single test didn’t even check lungs heart ears throat and you mean to tell me go home and give a 5 year old shots of turmeric honey and ginger ARE YOU JOKING after waiting 27 HOURS 🤦🏻‍♀️ Listen I don’t want my child on medication unless absolutely necessary but I’d like to know why out of nowhere my child couldn’t breathe at the very least, maybe check her lungs and heart, run some tests I dunno. Anyway we ended up leaving cause that’s it there isn’t any more paediatricians in. I went straight to a walk in clinic he checked everything he could sent for immediate blood work, X-ray and even ultrasound.all that was done in an hour and thank the lord it was because there was fluid in the lungs then to see a specialist to find out it was heart related. Also no I can’t sue the hospital or the dr I can report that dr but I’m just glad everything is okay and I trusted my mom gut and getting the appropriate care. Like I said in the beginning before going off and venting my story I would still choose all of that to have health care for free because imagine I got a dr like that in the states would have to pay for that then also pay for the dr, tests, specialist and more tests that would be insane even if it happened quicker no thank you. I’ll choose the free crappy healthcare for situations as such because that dr could have very easily been a dr in the States right. If you got this far I appreciate YOU and I truly appreciate you caring enough to read it to the end. May this year bring you everything you deserve and more!!!

text:
To anyone interested enough to read this:

First of all, I have to say that I couldn't be more grateful to be born in Quebec, Canada. I was a child who was not supposed to survive. My parents were told I was supposed to die at 5 yo and then 10 yo after that. I'm 29 now and still going strong. 

Most of my childhood souvenirs I have are the ones where I was hospitalized. I was 6 months per years at least in the hospital. Having an unknown genetic condition, I had to go through a battery of tests from skin autopsy to lumbar puncture and others. At one time, my bladder collapsed on herself, and in 2004, they were able to reconstruct it. I was on the operation table for 10 hours and was the first kid to ever receive this operation in Quebec. Again, they never found out what cause it. I could tell you many others story about different operation  or health problem I had to go through, but the most important thing is that my family was still able to keep their head over the water financially. 

My mother had to stop working so she could be with me at the hospital and for all the different appointments. Luckily, my father had a good salary, so they could afford it it(that's not the case for all sick children). But in no world would they have been able to go through it without our healthcare system. 

I was deemed not insurable by private company, so my parents would have been in serious debt without it. Say goodbye to all the sports, music, or vacation you want to do. We are talking about millions of dollars of debt. You have to take into account a private teacher so I could keep up at school, a nannies to take care of my two sisters and many other things.

Today, I only need to go to the clinic once a year for a medical follow-up. I am one of the lucky ones! It would never have happened if it were not for all the great doctor and specialist who work and study my case so they could find a treatment to my condition.

Second of all, as a society in Canada, we pay taxes, so the kids in need the most are able to have access to these services. This is what Canada is all about, you pay for the less fortunate, and these are the sacrifices you need to be willing to do when you live north of the border. The common good before the individual good, so every kid can have a chance. You may never have to go to the hospital before the birth of your child or your first prostate exam, and that's okay!

Our system is not perfect, and we do need to keep pushing the politicians to do better jobs, but I would never trade it to a private system. 

The US and Canada are fundamentally different in their ways of thinking. In the States, it's more individual than it is over here, and that's okay! But I don't wish anyone to go through what I lived in a country with no health insurance or private company that won't insure you.

Finally, to anyone telling me that we are heavily taxes, I just want to say that I'm a mining engineer now, and I give more than half my salary each year to the government before deduction. Even if I don't go to the hospital as often now and I dont agree with all the government decisions, I still think it's worth it. It is for the good of society as a whole. It might help save a child that I will probably never meet, so he can grow up and make this world a better place.

Thank you to anyone who got that far 🙏

text:
I got pneumonia on my trip to Europe earlier this year. My mother had just gotten off of a day shift when my stepdad woke me up and told her that I was feverish, not eating, and was sleeping insane amounts (we’re talking 16+ hours a day). She took me to the ER and we got in right away when I told the triage nurse that I was having some trouble breathing. Got a day’s worth of antibiotics without any charge, and was on the mend and eating the next morning. Recently went in because my youngest sister thought that she had fractured her arm. Was busier and we only waited 30 minutes before we were brought into the back. Love Canada. 

Right now, my best friend in the States is struggling with pneumonia and getting him to go to the doctor was like pulling teeth from a 5 year old. Absolute nightmare. Now he’s currently dealing with a second student loan just to pay for the ER visit.

text: I've worked in healthcare in the USA for 15 years and I've seen a lot. I worked in the ER for 10 of those years and the majority of patients have private insurance. Those in poverty that do not have private insurance are usually covered by social programs such as Medicare and Medicaid. Those that fall in the middle, even if they do have private insurance, but have medical bills they can't pay, can apply for grants and financial aid. I've been a recipient of the aid myself. Most people are just too lazy to fill out the paperwork required. I pay less than $50 a month for my health insurance premiums, which include dental and vision, and all preventative care and tests are free. They actually pay ME to get preventative care. I also worked for an Orthopedic Surgeon for years and people would get angry if they had to wait a week to get in. Most times we could get them in within a few days. USA Healthcare definitely isn't perfect, but I much prefer it, as we have some of the most advanced care in the world.

It's not just waiting in the emergency room in public run systems, it's waiting for everything. I had very obvious cancer symptom and in Canada it took 6 weeks from going to the doctor to the point surgery was done. In the US from reading various forums, others had about 2 to 5 business days. Usually your healthcare is paid in the US by your employer or your private insurance, but even if it isn't, paying a lot beats dying. Cancer is a time bomb, wait times hurt and kill more.

text: at the doctors office i have never waited more then 10 minutes....at the ER you should expect to wait up to 1- 10 hours.. anything internal that cant be seen or isnt exposed is usually handled right away..so if you have a broken arm youll get some pain meds and be told to wait but if you said you also took a blow to your stomach or chest or head and youll probally be moved up the list for urgency..most of the time people in the ER shouldnt be in the ER and they fill up the place and waste so much time.. biggest thing in ER.. be polite, dont embelish or cause a scene this is how you are remembered and labeled in computer and youll prob be passed over.. most injuries you can tell what is going on and they are not stupid.. i got impailed in the back of my chest plate about 4 inch by 1 inch gash about an inch deep and when i got there i politely asked her to help me remove my shirt so she could see it and i kept my shirt like half on kinda sitting ontop of my shoulder so it was now visible and my skin was exposed i said it was really hard to get my shirt back on and she said dont worry just come ot this room take ur shirt off and i walked in to the room and the doctor stitched me up in under 15 mins.. took another hour tho cause ended up having a broken wrist and broken wrist and hand.. also remember so many of these people are brought in by ambulance or police escorted, that will usually take first come first serve of rooms, beds, attention..damn this is long AF

text: I've experienced both the US system and the big daddy of national healthcare (the NHS). I think one of the fundamental differences of emergency care is the distinction between ER and UC, whereas, A&E does both. I have found the ER to be very swift, but UC (which is what a lot of A&E cases are) is VERY comparable to A&E. Aside from emergency care, specialist care wait times in nationalised systems suck balls. Overall, I actually do prefer the US system as the end user, but it is WAY too expensive, both on your wallet, and on the taxpayer (because contrary to popular belief, huge amounts of taxes are used to pay for shortfalls from people who pay insurance, as well as those who can't afford to pay).

text: The key in the USA is to get a good paying job that offers great insurance. I worked for a supermarket thru college. There was nothing taken out of my paycheck for the insurance premiums and I only had to pay $100 per year for a deductible before the insurance covered 90% of my healthcare costs. Preventative stuff like mammograms, PAP smears, colonoscopy, physicals were free. Meds were $10 for name brand and $5 for generic. I could go to any specialist I wanted without seeing my Primary Care Physician first. I've never had better insurance since then, and I even work in health care now. My husband works for non-profit type companies and his insurance is equal to what I had at the supermarket.

text: In Canada, you COULD wait a long time, and in the U.S., you COULD pay through the nose. However, in both countries, it's not like that for everybody or even necessarily most people. However, the Canadian system may be slightly better because it is considerably more economical overall due to lower administrative costs, lower drug costs, and less profit. In the U.S., there is a lot of paperwork for hundreds of different insurance companies, drug costs are higher, and much of the system is a for-profit industry with some fairly high margins.

text:
Nah people don't realize they make you wait forever in the US too. It's just a myth that it's longer on average in Canada. The wait times depend on area, time of day, and who's in front of you. In both countries. For example, the average ER wait in Canada is between 3-8 hours. And it is the same in the US.

The only exception of course is if you are mega-loaded in the US (multimillionaire, not like a lawyer), then you are likely to have your own personal doctor on call who will see you whenever you want. Also VIPs like politicians will get priority treatment and will even reserve entire hospital floors.

But that's probably not what most people are talking about when they compare the two countries' healthcare.

text:
My friend nipped across the border to go to a Trader Joe's for an item she likes that they sell.
She fainted in the store. She has a pre-existing condition. She goes to the states frequently bc she has a bf there, so she is not an idiot and has an extra insurance rider on her BCAA ins. She also carries a card stating her condition and info about it with her I.D.
Someone at the grocery store called 911, she was taken to hospital, they gave her every treatment/test including a CT scan- and she was not fully conscious. When she came to, she realized what was going on, put a stop to any further treatments. She called her bf to bring copies of her ins documents. She called the claim number, and that is when she found out that she was not covered.
Long story short highlights:
 Turns out, her policy, which was supposed to auto-renew, had lapsed due to an error, she was never notified, and now she owes $10, 000.00 US, to the hospital!
 They were not going to let her out of that hospital without a substantial payment.
 Worked out to she was charged $25 A MINUTE that she was in there, and the itemized bill was insane. They charged  outrageous sums for every little thing, such as $8 cotton swabs.
 They claim that despite her info about her condition, which they obviously read as per certain notations on her paperwork, they had a "duty of care and caution"....
but some of the tests and procedures they did had NOTHING to do with a syncope (fainting) episode. The CT scan, for example, as she did not have any kind of head injury. Heart tests despite her heartrate being normal.
Etc.
They diagnosed her with an internal hernia, again, not having a thing to do with her fainting.

The U.S. system is a scam.

text:
This is not correct in Canada. We pay for our healthcare through our taxes. So an American person will bring home their paycheck and put it in their pocket and buy food in groceries and pay for their mortgage and rent Car🎉lo.  And they will have a little bit of money left over after they've paid all their bills. In Canada, there is nothing left. And you have to use your Visa card. So even though you walk into the a doctor's appointment and not have to pay. You still have to wait hours upon hours before you can get in. In fact, I have to wait 2 weeks before I can see my regular doctor to make an appointment.  And if I need to see someone sooner. I have to go to a walk in clinic. Which again, you wait up to about 3 hours, sometimes just an hour..If you're lucky ...back in 2010 even all the way up to 2015 , I had to race to the doctor's appointment office before anyone else would get there,in blizzard conditiond with my kids say 7 in the morning.  Wait till the moment they open up. Be the first one in the door put your name on a list, and if you were on the list, , they would phone you when you were to come in to have your appointment that day. And when I did do that. There were people in cars sick waiting throwing up in their car's some sick on the side of the road because they got dropped off their waiting in line, thinking that they would get in but get turned away. One girl sat in our car waiting for her dad to come back and get her..before the pandemic... Then I have to go to emergency and wait 9 hours.  You heard me right 9 hours. So this little cute skit is absolutely derranged. I actually have a horror story about the ER. In grand prairie Alberta, Canada. Where my husband was mistaken for a drug seaker. The nurse on call thought we were just in there for drugs. But actually, he had a blood clot in his lung because he showed no symptoms or signs of fever like pneumonia and so on. She thought we were just drug hounds. So she left us there and didn't give a s***!!!!!
And we had to wait 5 hours just to get into that room. And then another 3 hours he got a bed.  That nurse, she didn't even talk to us. She never took as vitals Nothing until he had an attack. He'd gone to the bathroom and walked back to bed. And the stress on his lung from doing that, he could hardly breathe. And I thought he was having a heart attack. I actually didn't even know that she was our nurse. I went to a different nursing station and they came and helped me. And she come along and she was like...." I saw you go to the bathroom, just find. What did you take when you were in there? You were fine when you walked out of the bathroom, what did you take? "....WALK FINE???! HE HAD TO GRAB THE WALLS AND RAILING TO GET BACK TO THE ROOM!!!! Well, he never left for 4 days after that. Because they finally did A CT scan with dYe and found out that he had a blood clot in his lung!  What is also interesting about the story? Is that we had gone in there 2 weeks? Previous with the exact same issue. And the SAME doctor did A CT scan. But he didn't use DYE.  and he said. Oh, looks like you have pneumonia,THE FIRST TIME and it was wrong.(9 hour wait that time too) He just didn't use dye!!!if he had used the dye, he would have found out that he had a blood clot In his lung. I guess it was a good thing. It was the same doctor that seen him previous and also looked at his charts. That nurse was a LAZY EXCUSE OF A HEALTHCARE WORKER!

text:
What a lie. Dual citizen here and uh...the majority of the US (over 80% has employer insurance (similar to Bismarck models in Japan, Germany, France). 
Public sector programs are indeed funded by tax dollars: Medicaid, State Chip, ACA, Veterans and portions of Medicare. Yes, I'm an advocate of streamlining these and having non profit insurance orgs administer them as done in other countries that use Bismarck models for their public sector. 

Lost my mom in BC Canada to Cancer last year and the wait times for the diagnostic imaging were horrendous. She was a nurse her entire life who paid her taxes for over 50 years and didn't get to see the Oncologist even once before dying. It was like a 3rd world country.

Canada's system is collapsing right now and is so bad its being dragged to the Supreme Court of Canada as they have had 30 years to fix the crisis, yet nothing changes. Public sector needs: patient focused funding instead of block payments, user fees, cut the administrative bloat and move to a Bismarck model with non-profit health insurance orgs. 
Last - allow the private sector to fully run alongside the public sector. It's the 21st century and Canada is now the last remaining country on planet earth that makes it illegal for its citizens to purchase private health insurance for ALL services covered under Provincial Medicare. Canadian citizens deserve what every other social democracy provides their citizens: A CHOICE.

text:
When discussing these topics, you have to go into detail, and I don't think you can't get political. Because a lot of the wait time issues in Canada come down to the government not building new hospitals, etc for a growing population. 

The USA is such an un-equal place for healthcare, that you have people waiting longer in the ER than in Canada in some cases. Then you have people waiting 20 min at for profit ERs that slightly cook the books by sending in a doctor or nurse to say hello, and count that as seeing the doctor. 

It's a very complexe system there, and for profit systems can manipulate the data. 

Not to mention the millions that don't go to the ER, because they dont have insurance. Or the thousands of places that have lost a hospital in the USA, because it is not profitable. Those all play into ER access. It's hard to have a ER wait time when there's no hospital to go to. 

I can tell you from people I know, sometimes Canada gets the new drug first. That is just approval processes. 

What I do know is that health systems across the world, including the USA are suffering under strain. 
Be they Canadian single tier, or two tier like Australia and the UK. People are waiting everywhere in ERs for non urgent cases. 

Also discuss what you are doing to rectify the crisis. A ER doctor in Hamilton redesigned how the ER at St Josephs works. They consistently have wait times under one hour, while neighboring ERs have 5 hour waits. Why is that practice not being expanded to other ERs?

text:
1. Americans won't permit or accept healthcare rationing. America spends much more for care in the last 6 months of life than any other country. By a lot.

Walk through any ICU and you'll routinely see some people in vegetative conditions with virtually no chance of getting better. Yet they are receiving a full court press with anything and everything that can be done from the standpoint of medical technology.

Some of the push for this care comes from families who are adamant against witholding or withdrawing care. But doctors are also guilty of over-treatment. We all know some crazy doctors who believe that miracles can happen daily in the ICU regardless of how hopeless the situation may be.

The expense is enormous and American families are divorced from it. If the patient is uninsured, the costs are absorbed by the treating hospital and the doctors. If the patient is insured, the deductible has been met and the rest is paid for by insurance or via the taxpayers (Medicaid/Medicare). 28% of Medicare costs involve the last 6 months of life. That is a ton of money.

You won't see this in any other country with a national health service. Elderly patients don't get started on dialysis in the NHS in England. They die of kidney failure. You won't see 75 or 80 year olds getting quadruple bypass surgery either. Cancer patients in Canada and England routinely don't get $10,000 a month drugs for cancer treatment that prolongs life by several months (on average). The drugs either aren't available at all like in Canada (where some cancer drugs never get approved), or they are made virtually unavailable through a lottery in England.

Americans are often over-treated. NHS patients are often under-treated.

2. Americans pay much more for pharmaceuticals than anyone else in the world. The American government, under pressure from the Big Pharma lobby, refuses to negotiate discount rates for Medicare. So Americans pay 30% to 300% more than Canadians for the identical drug made in New Jersey or California.

3. There is gigantic waste and fraud in Medicaid and Medicare programs. Billing is electronic and payment is automatic. Auditing is very expensive and apparently very rarely occurs. It's so lucrative and easy to skim hundreds of millions of dollars per scheme that organized crime is now involved.

There are reasonable estimates that the fraud is $120-$180 billion a year. This are astronomic numbers.

Comments are frequently made about how efficient Medicare and Medicaid are because they have such low administrative costs compared to insurance companies. (It's pretty difficult to scam insurance companies for large amounts of money.) But the administration of Medicare/Medicaid includes the Department of HHS and also various law enforcement arms of the federal government. That's not cheap. And it's apparently not very effective either.

And while every state struggles with Medicaid fraud, the Office of the Inspector General says the five topping the list are California, Texas, New York, Ohio and Kentucky. The good news is that states recovered $1.7 billion in fraudulent payments in 2011. The bad news is the government had to spend $208 million to do it.

4. Americans demand immediate access to technology. We want the ability to get an MRI or PET scan tomorrow or that latest diagnostic test and treatment reported on CNN. That kind of access is incredibly expensive both in overhead — to build the facilities and purchase the machines — let alone the costs of the procedures.

In no other country does this happen. Many small American cities have more MRI scans and PET scans than exist in entire Canadian provinces. In Canada and England there are waiting lists for these kinds of tests and they are often not approved because the yearly budget runs out of funds.

We Americans want these tests sometimes, even when they are considered not necessary for good health. We ask our doctors to rule out the 1 in 10,000 event. It's human nature to want the latest and greatest of everything. But its never free and this kind of medical care costs a tremendous amount of money.

"More than half of lower-back MRIs ordered at two Canadian hospitals were inappropriate or of questionable value for patients — and family doctors were more apt than other specialists to order these unnecessary tests, according to newly published medical research from Alberta and Ontario teams.  The findings are important because, in some parts of the country, MRI tests for the lower back account for about one-third of all MRI requests. Across the country, wait times for MRIs are long and patient access is limited."

Our friends in Canada and England accept rationing of healthcare sevices. Americans give no indication that they are willing to. Look at the uproar over the IPAB (called “death panels” by some) in the ACA law.

5. The wasteful practice of defensive medicine. In America there are enormous numbers of unnecessary tests and scans that are performed by a doctor to cover his/her ass just in case of a lawsuit. It's impossible to practice in America without facing the threat of litigation. I, and every doctor answering honestly, will admit to ordering defensive tests.

I've seen estimates of $600+ billion a year in unneeded tests and procedures done primarily to avoid litigation. That's a lot of money that could otherwise go to insuring the uninsured. Opponents to tort reform quote very small numbers because they only use the cost of defending lawsuits in their equation. But that's a drop in the bucket compared to what doctors in America waste so that no one will accuse them of "missing something."

6. Regulation and certification. The regulatory process in America has become so burdensome for hospitals, clinics and physicians that to meet compliance standards, a small army of administrators and attorneys are needed to go through them. After the ACA was implemented, many doctors had to join healthcare systems to navigate them. Over the past 10 years, our clinic administrative staff more than doubled. Similarly the hospital administrative staff have increased as well to meet regulatory requirements and pass the various inspections that accompany them. There are numerous fees and licenses involved in medical care today. There is a financial cost to this that gets passed on to insurance payers.

David Chan, MD (UCLA), hematology/oncology (Stanford University)

text:
Canadian here. Some facts are wrong in this video or simply portrayed in a better light than reality. 

1) Taxes are only 22% = FALSE - Hospitals are of provincial jurisdiction, therefore, some of the provincial taxes we pay for also go towards healthcare, not just federal (which would be an added state tax in the US, I'm guessing). Depending on your income bracket, you have to pay a combined taxes (provincial and federal) between 27.53% and 53.31% of your income in Québec province, which is generally around 40% for the middle class. Don't quote me on this part, but I've read that 22% of medical costs is covered by the federal taxes and around 30-40% is covered by provinces taxes. 

2) People are not seen based on how much money they have = FALSE - There is private doctor clinics where you can go to be seen faster and get better and faster care that is still fully or partially funded by public funds but you also have to pay additionnal fees. 

3) 80% of Canadians are satisfied with the healthcare system = FALSE - A recent study shows that around 66% of Canadians are satisfied (54% OK / 12% very). This varies greatly by region hovering between 54% (Saskatchewan/Manitoba) and 74% (Ontario) depending on the province. 

One thing that keeps coming up however is that 90% of Canadians would like improvement in the healthcare system. 

Lastly, from personnal experience. 

Took 4 years to get a family doctor who then decided to go private and had to wait another year and a half totalling 5 year and a half. 

After getting said family doctor, he only works 3 days a week and it takes around 3 months to get an appointment, and this is when they decide to return your calls. Had to call 3 times and go there in person as they never returned calls. Secretary was then mad at me for not calling, go figure..

Recently waited 12 hours in emergency room for a broken wrist (deemed not a priority). Only one doctor available per shift per hospital will do that.. 

One good thing is they recently created a website where you can get an appointment usually within 24h/48h within a certain radius around your residence with a random doctor, as an ''in-between'' solution. 

One thing for sure is that it's not all sunshine and rainbow as portrayed in this video.
video_title: Episode 3: How Does Health Care Work in Canada?

text: The comments here need to be taken with a grain of salt.  The reality is no system is perfect, and apples should be compared to apples.  Working in the Canadian Medical World, what I know for a fact is that we are complacent due to how good we have it.  Wait times-- so sick of hearing about this.  If people are willing to commute they have shorter wait times, meaning if you live in Toronto, and the wait time is a vast shorter wait time 30 mins north of the city, many patients opt to wait for something in "Toronto" so if you are willing to commute shorter wait times (BTW I am not talking about hours).  If you need any urgent surgeries there is NO wait time, I stress urgent.  Someone suffering with 15 years of knee pain wanting to suddenly have "urgent surgery" can wait a month or two.  Also please note many strides have been made post covid that removes minor surgeries from hospitals to non-hospital settings (still covered by provincial healthcare coverage) that will allow for hospitals to focus on more serious types of care.  The area I think the government needs to focus on is "how to use our healthcare system the right way" ----- that should be part of our school system, immigration system and work environments.  People do not know how good they have it until it is taken away.  People "dying" waiting, I don't buy that unless there is a full autopsy of what happened, people can die waiting at home for an ambulance to come to their home, but playing devils advocate, "did they call 911 too late?" how long did it take them to realize it was a serious issue (through no fault of their own)".....so let's look at the flip side.....how many people with no universal healthcare die b/c they won't go to the hospital worried about the bill?....I have to say feeling sick and being at my most vulnerable during that time, without saying "what is it going to cost me" is what I prefer.  So many other factors to struggle through life, I am glad I do not have to worry about taking a loved one to get medical care, and what it will cost me.  The reality of it (and a sad one) many countries that do not have universal care is b/c their country does not want to sacrifice spending allotted funds to other areas (military, pharma support, industrialism etc).  Keep in mind Pharma feeds into this whole "greedy" directive of sticking to private healthcare.  So as exciting at it would be to get an appointment with a dermatologist in 2 days to treat my eczema, I prefer waiting a few weeks and paying $0.00, to each their own.
video_title: Episode 3: How Does Health Care Work in Canada?

text: I see lots of other Canadians have commented, but when I saw this video I had to comment for any Americans thinking this is accurate. The only thing that was kind of accurate is you can pick your doctor, which is true if you can find one. There are more doctors and specialists available in the large cities, but if you are in a smaller town, rural area, or especially in one of our Maritime provinces it can be slim pickings. I am lucky because I live near Toronto, and have had my doctor for 30 years, but if he retires I will definitely be challenged to find a new doctor. I have family in three maritime provinces, and there are definitely major doctor and nurse shortages there. The tax claim is inaccurate as well if you look it up, if you make around 60k in Ontario vs California the Canadian will pay about 5k more in taxes. Plus, we have a separate health tax in Ontario, and if you are self-employed then you pay double the tax. Each province is in charge of the health care in their specific province, and they decide what treatments and drugs are available to the patients. As far as the wait times, it is true that if you are in a car accident and need surgery, you will be treated in a timely manner, but if you need a hip or knee replacement, the wait time to see a surgeon could be months, and then wait a few more months to get the surgery. A couple of my friends had sports injuries. The one was told the wait for his surgeon was going to be over a year. They both ended up going to Buffalo and paid for an MRI and treatment.

text:
Most Canadians don't realize that their country is now the only country on earth that makes it illegal for its citizens to purchase private health insurance for ALL services covered by their Provinces Medicare. The healthcare system is now being taken to the Supreme Court of Canada as it breaks the Charter of Rights and Freedoms for life, liberty...It is in the midst of a crisis. It happened in 2005 in Quebec vs Chaoulli and now it will soon happen with BC's Cambie Surgeries. 
Dual citizen here: born and raised in Canada. Used to be so proud of our UHC system but have since lived in Taiwan, S Korea, Ireland and now the States.  Taiwan and S Korea are always at the top of the charts for the worlds best health care system. However, they (and every other nation) allow a private sector to FULLY function alongside the public. It helps to prevent overwhelming the system. 
Learned first hand how much of a crisis it currently is in Canada. Listen, I'm glad when I hear of other peoples successful cancer stories, but my mother's isn't one of them. Passed Oct 2021 after being diagnosed with cancer in the summer. She died without seeing an Oncologist ONCE and without even a prognosis of how much time she had left, so she and all of us could get our head around it. The diagnostic imaging, endoscopies, tests were ridiculously far apart. So crazy as she was an RN her entire adult life in BC; paid her taxes faithfully into an inefficient and broken system that wasn't there for her when she needed it most. There are historic levels of people dying on waitlists or becoming addicted to drugs while they wait for their surgeries. 15% of Canadians cannot find a family GP.
Assuming Cambie surgeries wins, I hope Canadians will be allowed to purchase private health insurance for ALL services provided by their provinces medicare. You earn your hard earned dollars from your job and it should be YOUR choice how to spend it. If you choose to spend it on a health insurance company or to get a procedure done because the gov't is too slow - that should be your choice.

text:
Most individuals suffering from illness experience inequity.   No one life is worth more than another.  

I would like to share my private pay spinal surgery experience with you and Dr. Raza.    I paid for elective L4 L5 minimally invasive bilateral decompression surgery in Calgary September 25, 2019, costing over 16K with taxes.   I had a consultation with the surgeon costing 1300.00.   I sustained a fall and I had burning radiculopathy to the right foot and this pain increased with sitting resulting in right groin pain.  I could not sit down, lie down and I stood for far too long compromising my musculoskeletal system.   I provided the surgeon with a pelvic surgical report from 2 months prior his spinal surgery  indicating distended organs (cecum and gallbladder--both on the right side) and explained that the one of two nerve blocks targeted the pelvis area (before pelvic surgery for a rectocele repair) and the second nerve block skimmed the right foot with fleeting results.  

The surgeon advised me that the second nerve block serves as a "valuable diagnostic tool" indicating the source of pain is from the spinal level.  I bought it.   

I woke up from surgery with no benefit.  The surgeon advised me that he had to perform a discectomy "for free" sounding like a hero.  I was sick but attempted to be optimistic.  I returned to BC after staying with sister in Calgary for one month.  I failed to recover and paid for an MRI identifying the L3 L4 as the surgical site.  I paid for an L4 L5 and the clinician in BC suggested that all I really needed was a micro discectomy and this procedure is typically covered by healthcare. I relocated to Calgary and purchased an inexpensive home hoping to receive continuity of care a pursue graduate studies.

I gave the surgeon a copy of the MRI and he joked about having to check if he operated on the correct level.  He did not admit to surgically altering the L3 spinal processes.  He referred me to his private pay colleague for my sacroiliac joints.  I would learn from three healthcare providers in BC that my sacroiliac joints are fine when I had to return to ensure my father was transferred to a long term care facility.   Nothing wrong with my sacroiliac joints?  

I returned to my new home in Calgary and would discover that the surgeon did surgically alter the L3 spinal processes when I had an Xray of my spine.  I was uploading the image to HCPs in Europe for second opinion although I don't have money for such an expense and I am living on long term disability.   I spoke with the surgeon and I stated "I know something is wrong below my waist".  He was angry and discharged me as a patient due to lack of trust.   

I paid for a private MRI in Calgary that would identify sacral insufficiency fractures February 2020.  I was surprised with diagnosis because my symptoms were the same and worsening.  I requested a diagnostic review and the same private pay radiologist would review his own image as a public employee with Alberta health Services.   This is not a second opinion.

Several healthcare providers in Ontario see no evidence of these fractures and I continue to live with the symptoms that should have disappeared several months ago.  Sacral insufficiency fractures heal over time and my symptoms persist suggesting that I do not have  fractures leading me to ask "What was present in those February 2020 MRIs if they are not sacral insufficiency fractures?"  This is the million dollar question.  Whatever is present in the February 2020 MRIs may provide my clinicians with information regarding a diagnosis and a lead to the development of a care plan. 

I had multiple visits with HCPs and various reviews and several clinicians would say "Don't worry, Dr. X (the surgeon) will take care of everything." Quite frankly, I don't want his care if I feel he has not acted with integrity.   I went to ER departments  with this pain and would learn I have a right renal aneurysm.  "Aneurysms don't cause pain" I was advised by one ER doctor.   Well, these sacral fractures are not resolved. 

In addition, I was dissuaded to have EMG tests by the surgeon's office and these tests would indicate that my symptoms are worsening.  Why would I pay my surgeon's colleague for prolotherapy if no one can or will  identify the source of my symptoms?  I was not going to pay for unnecessary injections until I knew the source of my pain.  The surgeon's colleague, a physiatrist, performed an insincere right hip assessment in July 2020 and we know now from an Ultrasound from April 2021 and right hip MRI from January 2022 that I am living with the potential of  acetabular femoral impingement and right hip issues explaining, in part, my inability to sit, lie down and stand.  

I would have bone scans, CT scans, angios, X-rays, ultrasounds etc and I received anonymous emails in July 2021 berating me, my mental wellness, calling me stupid and outlining the surgical procedure in detail.   These emails were sent 8 months after I fled Alberta for impartial healthcare in Ontario.   I would contact the Faculty of Medicine at the University of  Calgary with every new report indicating that I am living with issues involving the spinal surgery performed September 25, 2019,  the surgical alteration of the L3 spinal processes  and identifying that the source of my symptoms were not spinal related.    The authors of these emails advised me that I was an undesirable patient and to "get a real spinal surgeon!" when I explained that I was advised that I have tethered nerves at the L3 L4 level.    I am very happy with the orthopaedic surgeon who is investigating my  spinal health concerns here in Ontario and I feel that he is acting in my best interest.  

I have had the benefit--yes...the benefit...of a neurogram and defecogram indicating pelvic issues anterior to the sacrum and sacral nerve issues posterior to the sacrum.    I have a dropping bladder--to the right (hmm could this be related to right groin and foot pain?)--a horizontal urethra and a telescopic bowel and  I am now living with right hip bursitis, rectus femoris and glut medius tendonosis coupled with a left hamstring tear with sacral nerve root ectasia  as a result of not having my issues investigated in a timely manner.    This is not a result of the aging process.  I am fit, small and engaged in group fitness classes regularly.  

I was redeemed when an Ontario clinician gently advised me:  "Do not be too hard on yourself...the surgeon had the responsibility to ensure adequate testing before surgery and after surgery when you failed to recover."  This did not happen in Calgary.   

Public healthcare is superior (perhaps not in Alberta) and I say this after consulting with the orthopaedic surgeon in Ontario who stated "I have to be sure of what is going on before any surgical intervention."   I was not asking for surgery and I am actually very afraid of making the same mistake twice coupled with living with this pain that has worsened since private pay surgery.  

We need to protect this public healthcare that I have experienced first hand in Ontario.   My experience in Calgary where I paid for ineffective and unnecessary spinal surgery has resulted in the use of tens of thousands of dollars of finite healthcare resources and I believe the authors of those berating anonymous emails know where the problems lies and considered me to be some sort of cash cow.   Yes, go ahead, call me a cow and I will reproduce emails and telephone conversations that all indicate that private pay spinal surgery was performed without adequate testing, was not helpful or needed, and served to create even more health concerns resulting in the use of much needed finite healthcare resources. 

The well has run dry.   Please keep public healthcare strong and pay our healthcare providers appropriately.

text:
Healthcare in BC is really messed up. I don't live in a rural area but it's still extremely difficult to get a family doctor ~ because they act as private businesses, they have no obligation to accept any particular patients (unless they're a new grad, but it's only a number req meaning they're even less likely to accept 'difficult' cases). I was born in BC and my family doctor dropped me at age 6. I'm 25 and still no family doctor, and have been waiting on the provincial program to connect me to an NP or GP for 6 years.
Many clinics now refuse to do paperwork because it's not covered - including referrals. This private/public thing is the core issue in my opinion. Doctors should just be paid a set hourly or yearly salary, hired by the local health authority. The hospitals aren't great but they are 1000x more functional than local private clinics! They also couldn't deny people or pick & choose who to serve; people could make appointments without having to be a designated patient under a specific doctor. They could then incentivize doctors to move out to rural areas with greater salary offerred as they do with nurses.

As well, I'm legally disabled and 'technically' qualify for those low income/disabled health supports for uninsured services....
Except they don't cover what it actually costs. I'm looking at a $2000+ bill to get my wisdom teeth removed; the gov only wants to cover about $600. Thats the low end average cost for 1 extraction; I need 4. Even with private insurance through my university (which I pay as part of my tuition), I'm still looking at a $1200 bill.
Many people's options in these scenarios is to wait until they develop a deadly abcess that threatens their life to have it surgically removed in the hospital for free. It costs them more than if they just provided the necessary preventative care through the dentists.

This extra time and the ability to delve deeper into such cases is extremely important for patients like me. I am not saying Canadian healthcare is useless or terrible or anything like that. text:
1st comment! Great analysis. The Dr shortage is definitely a major problem thats been leading to hallway medicine, packed waiting rooms and critical delays in pt care with tragic consequences (The 44 year old father in Alberta being the most recent). 

One of the major causes of the doctor shortage is that internationally trained Canadian doctors can't return home to practice because of lack of  residency spots. The highly coveted residency spots are being taken up by Saudi Arabian doctors that eventually return to Saudi Arabia per their contract agreements. This arrangement has been highly lucrative to the universities to the tune of $100,000 or more per Saudi resident but is costing the population dearly.

I think these spots need to be opened up so more Canadian Doctors that completed med school in the US, Australia, UK and around the world can return home and treat Canadian patients permanently.
